INTRODUCTION 
Welcome to the Foundational Believers’ Class. This program is not just a course, 
it
 
is
 
a
 
journey.
 
A
 
journey
 
that
 
will
 
lead
 
you
 
into
 
a
 
deeper
 
relationship
 
with
 
Christ,
 
a
 
stronger
 
foundation
 
in
 
your
 
faith,
 
and
 
a
 
life
 
that
 
reflects
 
His
 
character
 
in
 
all
 
you
 
do.
 
The path you are about to embark on is one that seeks to establish you in the core 
truths
 
of
 
the
 
Christian
 
faith,
 
nurturing
 
spiritual
 
maturity,
 
character
 
formation,
 
and
 
faithful
 
service.
 
This
 
is
 
not
 
merely
 
an
 
opportunity
 
to
 
gain
 
knowledge,
 
but
 
to
 
build
 
your
 
life
 
on
 
the
 
solid
 
foundation
 
of
 
Christ’s
 
Word
 
and
 
His
 
purposes
 
for
 
your
 
life.
 
Over the course of this year, you will be guided through systematic biblical 
instruction
 
that
 
will
 
strengthen
 
your
 
understanding
 
of
 
God’s
 
Word,
 
empower
 
you
 
to
 
walk
 
in
 
victory
 
over
 
sin,
 
and
 
equip
 
you
 
to
 
serve
 
effectively
 
in
 
His
 
Kingdom.
 
You
 
will
 
learn
 
to
 
embrace
 
your
 
identity
 
in
 
Christ
 
and
 
understand
 
your
 
vital
 
role
 
in
 
the
 
body
 
of
 
Christ.
 
This
 
journey
 
will
 
also
 
challenge
 
you
 
to
 
grow
 
in
 
your
 
spiritual
 
disciplines,
 
overcome
 
the
 
works
 
of
 
the
 
flesh,
 
and
 
develop
 
a
 
lifestyle
 
that
 
reflects
 
Christ’s
 
love,
 
humility,
 
and
 
power.
 
At the heart of this program is the goal of shaping you into a true disciple of 
Jesus,
 
a
 
believer
 
whose
 
life
 
radiates
 
His
 
love
 
and
 
truth.
 
By
 
the
 
end
 
of
 
this
 
course,
 
we
 
trust
 
that
 
you
 
will
 
be
 
firmly
 
rooted
 
in
 
sound
 
doctrine,
 
empowered
 
by
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit,
 
and
 
ready
 
to
 
serve
 
the
 
local
 
church
 
and
 
beyond,
 
living
 
with
 
joy,
 
purpose,
 
and
 
conviction.
 
As you begin this journey, know that it is a privilege to walk with you as you 
grow
 
in
 
faith,
 
obedience,
 
and
 
service.
 
You
 
are
 
laying
 
the
 
groundwork
 
for
 
a
 
lifelong
 
journey
 
of
 
discipleship,
 
where
 
you
 
will
 
continuously
 
grow
 
in
 
Christ,
 
live
 
for
 
His
 
glory,
 
and
 
fulfill
 
His
 
calling
 
on
 
your
 
life.
 
May this class be a turning point in your spiritual walk, and may you leave this 
year
 
stronger,
 
more
 
equipped,
 
and
 
more
 
passionate
 
about
 
serving
 
God
 
and
 
others.
 
Dr. Daniel Folarin Founder/Senior Pastor Great Haven Ministries Inc. 
3

 TERM 4: WORKERS IN TRAINING & MINISTRY

(Weeks

 46. Faithfulness in Little Things - The pathway to promotion (Luke

 Module 10: Practical Ministry & Evangelism (Weeks 48-52) 48. Preaching & Teaching Effectively - Preparing sermons/Bible

 51. Apologetics - Defending the Faith (1 Peter 3:15) 52. Final Week: Commitment & Graduation ● Recap of key lessons ● Personal testimonies ● Commissioning & charge

TERM 1: FOUNDATIONS OF THE FAITH (Weeks 1-15) Module 1: Understanding Salvation (Weeks 1-5) 
9

Believers' Class - Week 1 TOPIC: THE GOSPEL AND SALVATION - WHAT IT MEANS TO 
BE
 
SAVED
 INTRODUCTION 
Salvation
 
is
 
the
 
foundation
 
of
 
the
 
Christian
 
faith.
 
God’s
 
plan
 
is
 
to
 
rescue
 
humanity
 
from
 
sin
 
and
 
restore
 
fellowship
 
with
 
Him
 
through
 
the
 
preaching
 
of
 
the
 
gospel
 
(Romans
 
10:13-17).
 
As
 
the
 
Scriptures
 
declare
 
in
 
Romans
 
1:16-17,
 
the
 
gospel
 
is
 
the
 
power
 
of
 
God
 
unto
 
salvation
 
for
 
everyone
 
who
 
believes.
 
 In this lesson, we will explore the meaning of the gospel and salvation, 
how
 
they
 
are
 
received,
 
and
 
their
 
significance
 
for
 
the
 
believer.
 1. UNDERSTANDING THE GOSPEL The word gospel means "good news." The good news is that Jesus Christ 
came
 
to
 
save
 
mankind
 
from
 
sin
 
and
 
reconcile
 
us
 
to
 
God.
 The gospel is the central message of Christianity, yet many misunderstand 
it.
 
The
 
gospel
 
is
 
not
 
just
 
the
 
beginning
 
of
 
the
 
Christian
 
life
 
but
 
the
 
foundation
 
of
 
everything.
 
 Key Scriptures: ● John 3:16 - "For God so loved the world, that he gave his only 
begotten
 
Son,
 
that
 
whosoever
 
believeth
 
in
 
him
 
should
 
not
 
perish,
 
but
 
have
 
everlasting
 
life."
 ● Romans 5:8 - "But God commendeth his love toward us, in that, 
while
 
we
 
were
 
yet
 
sinners,
 
Christ
 
died
 
for
 
us."
 Key Points: 1. The gospel is centered on Jesus Christ - His birth, death, 
resurrection,
 
and
 
ascension.
 2. Sin separated man from God, but Jesus’ sacrifice provided a way 
back.
 
10

3. Salvation is a gift from God, not earned by good works (Ephesians 
2:8-9).
 
 The Finality of the Gospel of Christ ● The gospel of Christ defines humanity through its lens. ● The work of salvation is entirely the work of God. ● Faith in Christ is receiving from, not giving to Christ. ● The wisdom of the gospel does not rely on personalities but on 
Christ’s
 
finished
 
work.
 2. THE GOSPEL: NOT RELIGION, NOT IRRELIGION The gospel stands apart from both legalism (religion) and relativism 
(irreligion).
 Key Differences: ● Religion (Legalism): "I obey; therefore, I am accepted." ● Irreligion (Secularism): "I do what I want; there is no ultimate 
truth."
 ● The Gospel: "I am accepted through Jesus Christ; therefore, I 
obey."
 Key Scripture: Ephesians 2:8-9 - "For by grace are ye saved through 
faith;
 
and
 
that
 
not
 
of
 
yourselves:
 
it
 
is
 
the
 
gift
 
of
 
God:
 
Not
 
of
 
works,
 
lest
 
any
 
man
 
should
 
boast."
 The gospel is not about trying to earn God’s favor through good works, nor 
is
 
it
 
about
 
rejecting
 
moral
 
responsibility.
 
It
 
is
 
about
 
grace,
 
God’s
 
unmerited
 
favor
 
through
 
Christ.
 3. WHAT IT MEANS TO BE SAVED To be saved means to be delivered from sin and its consequences, to be 
made
 
new
 
in
 
Christ,
 
and
 
to
 
have
 
eternal
 
life
 
with
 
God.
 
11

Key Scriptures: ● Romans 10:9-10 - "That if thou shalt confess with thy mouth the 
Lord
 
Jesus,
 
and
 
shalt
 
believe
 
in
 
thine
 
heart
 
that
 
God
 
hath
 
raised
 
him
 
from
 
the
 
dead,
 
thou
 
shalt
 
be
 
saved."
 ● 2 Corinthians 5:17 - "Therefore if any man be in Christ, he is a new 
creature:
 
old
 
things
 
are
 
passed
 
away;
 
behold,
 
all
 
things
 
are
 
become
 
new."
 
 Key Points: 1. Salvation involves confessing Jesus as Lord and believing in His 
resurrection.
 2. It is a spiritual rebirth (John 3:3-5). 3. It grants access to God’s kingdom and eternal life. 
 4. HOW TO RECEIVE SALVATION Receiving salvation is a simple but profound act of faith. Steps to Salvation: 1. Hear the Gospel - Faith comes by hearing (Romans 10:17). 2. Repent of Sin - Acknowledge sin and turn away from it (Acts 
3:19).
 3. Believe in Jesus - Trust that He is the Son of God who died and 
rose
 
again.
 4. Confess Jesus as Lord - Openly declare your faith in Him 
(Romans
 
10:9-10).
 
 5. THE BENEFITS OF SALVATION ● Colossians 1:13 - "Who hath delivered us from the power of 
darkness,
 
and
 
hath
 
translated
 
us
 
into
 
the
 
kingdom
 
of
 
his
 
dear
 
Son."
 ● John 10:10 - "I am come that they might have life, and that they 
might
 
have
 
it
 
more
 
abundantly."
 
 Salvation brings many blessings, including: 1. Eternal Life - Assurance of heaven with God (John 5:24). 
12

2. Victory Over Sin - Freedom from the bondage of sin (Romans 
6:14).
 3. Peace with God - Restored relationship with the Father (Romans 
5:1).
 4. Indwelling of the Holy Spirit - Guidance and empowerment for 
righteous
 
living
 
(Acts
 
2:38).
 5. Membership in God's Family - The believer becomes a child of 
God,
 
and
 
then
 
it
 
qualifies
 
you
 
for
 
membership
 
in
 
the
 
body
 
of
 
Christ
 
(John
 
1:12,
 
1
 
Cor.
 
12:13).
 
 6. LIFE AFTER SALVATION - THE BELIEVER’S WALK Salvation is not the end but the beginning of a new life in Christ. Key Responsibilities of a Saved Person: 1. Grow Spiritually - Study God’s Word and pray daily (2 Timothy 
2:15).
 2. Fellowship with Other Believers - Be part of a local church 
(Hebrews
 
10:25).
 3. Live a Holy Life - Walk in righteousness and obedience (1 Peter 
1:15-16).
 4. Share the Gospel - Tell others about Jesus (Mark 16:15). 
 CONCLUSION Salvation is the greatest gift from God to humanity. It is by grace through 
faith
 
in
 
Jesus
 
Christ.
 
If
 
you
 
have
 
not
 
yet
 
received
 
this
 
gift,
 
today
 
is
 
the
 
day
 
of
 
salvation
 
(2
 
Corinthians
 
6:2).
 
Those
 
who
 
are
 
saved
 
must
 
keep
 
growing
 
in
 
the
 
knowledge
 
of
 
God
 
and
 
walking
 
in
 
the
 
light
 
of
 
His
 
Word.
 ASSIGNMENT 1. Memorize Romans 10:9-10 and John 3:16 . 2. Write a short testimony of how you received salvation. 3. Share the gospel with at least one person this week.

Believers' Class - Week 2 TOPIC: The Gospel is Not Just the ABCs But the A to Z Key Scripture: Colossians 2:6-7 
 
"As
 
ye
 
have
 
therefore
 
received
 
Christ
 
Jesus
 
the
 
Lord,
 
so
 
walk
 
ye
 
in
 
him."
 Introduction: Many people think that the gospel is just the entry point into the Christian 
life,
 
but
 
as
 
Timothy
 
Keller
 
emphasized,
 
the
 
gospel
 
is
 
not
 
just
 
the
 
beginning;
 
it
 
is
 
the
 
foundation
 
of
 
the
 
entire
 
Christian
 
life.
 
It
 
shapes
 
everything
 
about
 
us,
 
from
 
how
 
we
 
see
 
ourselves
 
to
 
how
 
we
 
interact
 
with
 
the
 
world.
 In this lesson, we will explore how the gospel impacts every aspect of the 
believer's
 
life,
 
and
 
how
 
it
 
shapes
 
our
 
salvation,
 
sanctification,
 
mission,
 
and
 
relationships.
 1. The Gospel Shapes Everything The gospel is the power of God unto salvation (Romans 1:16). When we 
believe
 
the
 
gospel,
 
we
 
receive
 
salvation
 
by
 
faith,
 
not
 
by
 
our
 
works.
 
It’s
 
the
 
beginning
 
of
 
a
 
new
 
life
 
in
 
Christ.
 
Romans
 
10:9-10
 
-
 
"That
 
if
 
thou
 
shalt
 
confess
 
with
 
thy
 
mouth
 
the
 
Lord
 
Jesus,
 
and
 
shalt
 
believe
 
in
 
thine
 
heart
 
that
 
God
 
hath
 
raised
 
him
 
from
 
the
 
dead,
 
thou
 
shalt
 
be
 
saved."
 Salvation is God’s gift , not an exchange. The unsaved person has nothing 
to
 
give;
 
they
 
are
 
dead
 
in
 
sin.
 
But
 
salvation
 
is
 
about
 
receiving
 
God's
 
life
 
through
 
Christ’s
 
resurrection.
 Sanctification: The Gospel Changes Us Sanctification is the ongoing transformation into the image of Christ. The 
gospel
 
is
 
not
 
just
 
a
 
past
 
event;
 
it
 
is
 
the
 
present
 
power
 
that
 
shapes
 
our
 
character.
 
2
 
Corinthians
 
3:18
 
-
 
"But
 
we
 
all,
 
with
 
open
 
face
 
beholding
 
as
 
in
 
a
 
14

glass the glory of the Lord, are changed into the same image from 
glory
 
to
 
glory,
 
even
 
as
 
by
 
the
 
Spirit
 
of
 
the
 
Lord."
 The gospel continually shapes us, transforming us into the likeness of 
Christ.
 
The
 
gospel
 
is
 
not
 
just
 
for
 
the
 
beginning
 
of
 
the
 
Christian
 
journey
,
 
but
 
for
 
the
 
whole
 
journey.
 Mission: The Gospel Compels Us The gospel compels us to share it. It is the good news that the world 
desperately
 
needs.
 
We
 
are
 
not
 
saved
 
to
 
stay
 
passive
 
but
 
to
 
be
 
active
 
participants
 
in
 
God's
 
mission
.
 
Matthew
 
28:19-20
 
-
 
"Go
 
ye
 
therefore,
 
and
 
teach
 
all
 
nations,
 
baptizing
 
them
 
in
 
the
 
name
 
of
 
the
 
Father,
 
and
 
of
 
the
 
Son,
 
and
 
of
 
the
 
Holy
 
Ghost..."
 The gospel gives us a mission to bring the lost to Christ, to make disciples, 
and
 
to
 
teach
 
others
 
about
 
the
 
truth
 
of
 
the
 
resurrection.
 Social Transformation: The Gospel Renews Culture and Society The gospel is the power that can renew culture and transform society . It 
calls
 
us
 
to
 
work
 
for
 
justice,
 
mercy,
 
and
 
righteousness
 
in
 
our
 
world.
 
 
Luke
 
4:18-19
 
-
 
"The
 
Spirit
 
of
 
the
 
Lord
 
is
 
upon
 
me,
 
because
 
he
 
hath
 
anointed
 
me
 
to
 
preach
 
the
 
gospel
 
to
 
the
 
poor..."
 The gospel has social implications, it demands that we love our neighbors, 
care
 
for
 
the
 
poor,
 
and
 
work
 
for
 
justice.
 
It
 
renews
 
society
,
 
bringing
 
God's
 
kingdom
 
to
 
bear
 
on
 
every
 
area
 
of
 
life.
 2. The Gospel Changes Our Identity The gospel doesn’t just save us; it transforms our identity . Instead of 
defining
 
ourselves
 
by
 
our
 
achievements,
 
our
 
relationships,
 
or
 
our
 
performance,
 
we
 
find
 
our
 
true
 
identity
 
in
 
Christ.
 Gospel Identity vs. Other Identities ● Performance-Based Identity: "I am valuable because of what I 
do."
 
15

● People-Pleasing Identity: "I am valuable because others approve 
of
 
me."
 ● Gospel Identity: "I am valuable because God loves me in Christ." 
 
Galatians
 
2:20
 
-
 
"I
 
am
 
crucified
 
with
 
Christ:
 
nevertheless
 
I
 
live;
 
yet
 
not
 
I,
 
but
 
Christ
 
liveth
 
in
 
me."
 When we embrace gospel identity , we find our worth in what Christ has 
done
 
for
 
us,
 
not
 
in
 
what
 
we
 
do
 
for
 
ourselves
 
or
 
what
 
others
 
think
 
of
 
us.
 3. The Gospel Transforms Relationships The gospel is the power of reconciliation . It reconciles us to God, and it 
also
 
reconciles
 
us
 
to
 
one
 
another.
 
The
 
gospel
 
teaches
 
us
 
forgiveness,
 
humility,
 
and
 
sacrificial
 
love.
 How the Gospel Transforms Relationships: ● Forgiveness: Because we are forgiven, we forgive others 
(Ephesians
 
4:32).
 ● Humility: Because we are saved by grace, we don’t feel superior to 
others
 
(Philippians
 
2:3-4).
 ● Love: Because Christ loved us first, we love others sacrificially (1 
John
 
4:19).
 
 
 
2
 
Corinthians
 
5:18
 
-
 
"And
 
all
 
things
 
are
 
of
 
God,
 
who
 
hath
 
reconciled
 
us
 
to
 
himself
 
by
 
Jesus
 
Christ,
 
and
 
hath
 
given
 
to
 
us
 
the
 
ministry
 
of
 
reconciliation."
 The gospel reconciles us to God, and this reconciliation enables us to live 
in
 
peace
 
and
 
unity
 
with
 
others.
 
We
 
live
 
by
 
grace
,
 
not
 
by
 
superiority
 
or
 
judgment.
 4. The Gospel Calls Us to Serve and Renew the World The gospel is not just about personal salvation but about restoring all of 
creation
.
 
As
 
believers,
 
we
 
are
 
called
 
to
 
serve
 
the
 
world.
 Gospel-Centered Mission: ● Care for the poor and marginalized (James 1:27) 
16

● Work for justice and righteousness (Micah 6:8) ● Share the gospel with the world (Matthew 5:14-16) 
 
 
Luke
 
4:18
 
-
 
"The
 
Spirit
 
of
 
the
 
Lord
 
is
 
upon
 
me,
 
because
 
he
 
hath
 
anointed
 
me
 
to
 
preach
 
the
 
gospel
 
to
 
the
 
poor..."
 Conclusion: Living in the Gospel The gospel is not just something we believe ; it is something we live . It 
shapes
 
our
 
identity
,
 
our
 
relationships
,
 
and
 
our
 
mission
 
in
 
the
 
world.
 Practical Application: 1. Preach the gospel to yourself daily : Remember that you are saved 
by
 
grace,
 
not
 
works.
 2. Let the gospel shape your identity : Rest in Christ’s love rather 
than
 
worldly
 
achievements.
 3. Apply the gospel to your relationships : Live with humility, 
forgiveness,
 
and
 
love.
 4. Share the gospel with others : Be bold in proclaiming the good 
news.
 5. Live on mission : Seek justice, serve the needy, and be a light in 
your
 
community.
 
 
Romans
 
1:16
 
-
 
"For
 
I
 
am
 
not
 
ashamed
 
of
 
the
 
gospel
 
of
 
Christ:
 
for
 
it
 
is
 
the
 
power
 
of
 
God
 
unto
 
salvation
 
to
 
everyone
 
that
 
believeth."
 Questions: 1. How does the gospel shape your view of yourself? 2. In what ways has the gospel changed your relationships? 3. How can you better live out the gospel in your daily life? 
 Final Thought: 
The
 
gospel
 
is
 
not
 
about
 
what
 
we
 
do
 
for
 
God
 
but
 
what
 
He
 
has
 
done
 
for
 
us
 
in
 
Jesus
 
Christ.
 
Rest
 
in
 
His
 
grace,
 
and
 
let
 
it
 
transform
 
every
 
aspect
 
of
 
your
 
life.

Believers' Class: Week 3 The Assurance of Salvation: Can a Believer Be Sure of Their 
Salvation?
 
(1
 
John
 
5:11-13)
 
 Introduction: Have you ever doubted your salvation? The uncertainty about our standing 
with
 
God
 
can
 
be
 
a
 
heavy
 
burden
 
to
 
bear.
 
Maybe
 
you’ve
 
felt
 
that
 
any
 
unconfessed
 
sin
 
could
 
cost
 
you
 
your
 
eternity
 
with
 
God.
 
Maybe
 
you’ve
 
believed
 
that
 
no
 
matter
 
what
 
you
 
do,
 
if
 
Jesus
 
returns,
 
you’re
 
going
 
to
 
hell.
 
This
 
fear
 
and
 
uncertainty
 
can
 
rob
 
you
 
of
 
peace
 
and
 
joy
 
in

Believers' Class - Week 4 TOPIC: The Forgiveness of Sins Introduction One of the most important truths in the Christian faith is the forgiveness of 
sins.
 
Many
 
believers
 
struggle
 
with
 
whether
 
they
 
need
 
to
 
repeatedly
 
ask
 
for
 
forgiveness
 
in
 
every
 
prayer
 
or
 
if
 
their
 
sins
 
have
 
already
 
been
 
forgiven
 
in
 
Christ.
 
Understanding
 
the
 
finished
 
work
 
of
 
Christ
 
Jesus
 
will
 
help
 
us
 
walk
 
in
 
the
 
confidence
 
and
 
boldness
 
God
 
intends
 
for
 
us.
 The Basis of Our Forgiveness: The Blood of Jesus The forgiveness of sins is not based on our efforts, emotions, or repeated 
confessions.
 
It
 
is
 
based
 
entirely
 
on
 
the
 
sacrifice
 
of
 
Jesus
 
Christ.
 Ephesians 1:7 - “In whom we have redemption through his blood, 
the
 
forgiveness
 
of
 
sins,
 
according
 
to
 
the
 
riches
 
of
 
his
 
grace.”
 Colossians 1:14 - “In whom we have redemption through his blood, 
even
 
the
 
forgiveness
 
of
 
sins.”
 Matthew 26:28 - Jesus said, “For this is my blood of the new 
testament,
 
which
 
is
 
shed
 
for
 
many
 
for
 
the
 
remission
 
of
 
sins.”
 These scriptures show that the forgiveness of sins is not a future event but 
a
 
past
 
and
 
completed
 
act
 
through
 
Christ’s
 
shed
 
blood.
 
The
 
believer
 
is
 
already
 
forgiven.
 Forgiveness is a Completed Work The Bible teaches that forgiveness was accomplished once and for all by 
Christ’s
 
remission
 
of
 
our
 
sins.
 
This
 
means
 
that
 
upon
 
salvation,
 
all
 
our
 
sins,
 
past,
 
present,
 
and
 
future,
 
are
 
forgiven.
 Ephesians 4:32 - “And be ye kind one to another, tenderhearted, 
forgiving
 
one
 
another,
 
even
 
as
 
God
 
for
 
Christ’s
 
sake
 
hath
 
forgiven
 
you.”
 
22

The phrase “hath forgiven” indicates a completed action. A believer is not 
in
 
a
 
continuous
 
cycle
 
of
 
forgiveness
 
but
 
has
 
already
 
received
 
it
 
in
 
Christ.
 Hebrews 10:12-14 - “But this man, after he had offered one 
sacrifice
 
for
 
sins
 
forever,
 
sat
 
down
 
on
 
the
 
right
 
hand
 
of
 
God…
 
For
 
by
 
one
 
offering
 
he
 
hath
 
perfected
 
forever
 
them
 
that
 
are
 
sanctified.”
 Jesus’s sacrifice was once for all time, meaning we do not need repeated 
forgiveness
 
rituals.
 How Should a Believer Approach Sin? Although our sins are forgiven in Christ, believers are not called to live in 
sin.
 
The
 
Bible
 
encourages
 
a
 
lifestyle
 
of
 
righteousness
 
and
 
repentance
 
when
 
we
 
fall
 
short.
 1 John 1:9 - “If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to 
forgive
 
us
 
our
 
sins,
 
and
 
to
 
cleanse
 
us
 
from
 
all
 
unrighteousness.”
 This verse does not suggest that we need to continually seek new 
forgiveness.
 
Rather,
 
it
 
emphasizes
 
having
 
a
 
repentant
 
heart
 
and
 
acknowledging
 
when
 
we
 
fall
 
short.
 1 John 2:1-2 - “My little children, these things write I unto you, that 
ye
 
sin
 
not.
 
And
 
if
 
any
 
man
 
sin,
 
we
 
have
 
an
 
advocate
 
with
 
the
 
Father,
 
Jesus
 
Christ
 
the
 
righteous.”
 The goal is not to sin, but if we do, we already have an advocate in Jesus. Romans 6:1-2 - “What shall we say then? Shall we continue in sin, 
that
 
grace
 
may
 
abound?
 
God
 
forbid.
 
How
 
shall
 
we,
 
that
 
are
 
dead
 
to
 
sin,
 
live
 
any
 
longer
 
therein?”
 Forgiveness is not a license to sin, but an empowerment to live righteously. Do We Need to Ask for Forgiveness Every Time We Pray? Many believers have been taught that they must ask for forgiveness before 
every
 
prayer
 
or
 
else
 
God
 
will
 
not
 
hear
 
them.
 
However,
 
this
 
idea
 
is
 
inconsistent
 
with
 
the
 
Gospel.
 
23

Hebrews 4:16 - “Let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of 
grace,
 
that
 
we
 
may
 
obtain
 
mercy,
 
and
 
find
 
grace
 
to
 
help
 
in
 
time
 
of
 
need.”
 We are called to approach God with boldness, not guilt or fear of rejection. John 16:27 - “For the Father himself loveth you, because ye have 
loved
 
me,
 
and
 
have
 
believed
 
that
 
I
 
came
 
out
 
from
 
God.”
 God’s love for us is not dependent on constant pleas for forgiveness but on 
our
 
faith
 
in
 
Christ.
 1 John 3:20 - “For if our heart condemn us, God is greater than our 
heart,
 
and
 
knoweth
 
all
 
things.”
 Even when we feel unworthy, God’s assurance is greater than our feelings. Instead of repeatedly asking for forgiveness as though our sins are not yet 
forgiven,
 
we
 
should:
 1. Acknowledge our sins immediately when we fall 
(repentance
 
should
 
be
 
instant,
 
not
 
stored
 
for
 
prayer
 
time).
 2. Thank God for His forgiveness and embrace His grace. 3. Move forward in righteousness rather than wallowing in 
guilt.
 What About Answered Prayers? Some people believe that their prayers will not be answered if they have 
unconfessed
 
sins.
 
However,
 
the
 
Bible
 
teaches
 
that
 
God
 
answers
 
prayers
 
based
 
on
 
His
 
grace,
 
our
 
faith,
 
and
 
His
 
will,
 
not
 
our
 
perfection.
 Matthew 7:11 - “If ye then, being evil, know how to give good gifts 
unto
 
your
 
children,
 
how
 
much
 
more
 
shall
 
your
 
Father
 
which
 
is
 
in
 
heaven
 
give
 
good
 
things
 
to
 
them
 
that
 
ask
 
him?”
 A father does not stop feeding his child because the child made a mistake. 
24

John 14:13-14 - “And whatsoever ye shall ask in my name, that 
will
 
I
 
do,
 
that
 
the
 
Father
 
may
 
be
 
glorified
 
in
 
the
 
Son.
 
If
 
ye
 
shall
 
ask
 
anything
 
in
 
my
 
name,
 
I
 
will
 
do
 
it.”
 Our prayers are answered because of Jesus’ name and His righteousness, 
not
 
because
 
we
 
asked
 
for
 
forgiveness
 
beforehand.
 Final Exhortation: Living in the Assurance of Forgiveness Since our sins are already forgiven in Christ: ● We do not need to repeatedly ask for forgiveness in fear. ● We should confess our sins and repent immediately when we fall. ● We should approach God with boldness, knowing we are forgiven. ● We must grow in righteousness and avoid habitual sin. Key Takeaways: - Forgiveness is a finished work of Christ. - Believers do not need to ask for forgiveness every time they pray. - Confessing sin is about repentance and acknowledgment, not 
securing
 
fresh
 
forgiveness.
 - We approach God with boldness, not fear. - God answers prayers based on His love, not our perfection. Reflection Questions: 1. Do you approach God with confidence or with fear of rejection? 2. How can understanding forgiveness change your prayer life? 3. What should you do when you sin? 4. How can you balance living in grace and pursuing righteousness? Closing Prayer : Heavenly Father, thank You for the complete forgiveness I have in Christ. I 
receive
 
Your
 
grace
 
with
 
boldness,
 
knowing
 
that
 
my
 
sins
 
have
 
been
 
fully
 
dealt
 
with
 
by
 
Jesus’
 
sacrifice.
 
Help
 
me
 
to
 
live
 
righteously,
 
not
 
out
 
of
 
fear
 
but
 
out
 
of
 
love
 
for
 
You.
 
Thank
 
You
 
for
 
hearing
 
my
 
prayers
 
and
 
drawing
 
me
 
closer
 
to
 
You.
 
In
 
Jesus’
 
name,
 
Amen.
 
25

biblical

 The Role of John the Baptis t The word “Baptist” in John the Baptist’s name identifies his ministry of 
baptizing.
 
However,
 
John
 
was
 
primarily
 
a
 
prophet.
 Matthew 21:26 - “But if we shall say, Of men; we fear the people;

all
 
hold

 The primary role of an Old Testament prophet was to point to Christ, and 
John
 
did
 
this
 
through
 
his

his
 
message.
 - Figurative - It was symbolic of the coming Messiah. - Prophetic - It was a sign to confirm Jesus as the Christ (Mark 1:7-8, 
John
 
1:31-33).
 John’s baptism was known as the baptism of repentance (Matthew 3:1, 
Mark
 
1:4,
 
Acts
 
10:37,
 
Acts
 
13:24).
 
26

The Baptism of Jesus Jesus’ baptism was/is different from John’s baptism. Matthew 3:11 - “I indeed baptize you with water unto repentance: 
but
 
he
 
that
 
cometh
 
after
 
me

mightier
 
than
 
I,
 
whose
 
shoes
 
I
 
am
 
not
 
worthy
 
to
 
bear:
 
he
 
shall
 
baptize
 
you
 
with
 
the
 
Holy
 
Ghost,
 
and
 
with
 
fire.”
 Jesus never baptized anyone in water (John 3:22, John 4:2). Baptism Does Not Always Mean Water Baptism The Greek word baptizo means “to immerse” or “to place into,” and its 
meaning
 
depends
 
on
 
the
 
context.
 
Baptism
 
in
 
the
 
Bible
 
does

realities:

Jesus
 
Christ

Christ

put
 
on
 
Christ.”

one

 In these passages, baptism refers to being placed into Christ by faith, not 
into
 
water.
 Matthew 28:19-20 Reexamined “Go ye therefore, and teach all nations, baptizing them in the name 
of
 
the
 
Father,
 
and
 
of
 
the
 
Son,
 
and
 
of
 
the
 
Holy
 
Ghost.”
 However, “baptizing them” here does not necessarily mean water baptism. 
The
 
word
 
baptism
 
(Greek:
 
baptizo)
 
means
 
“to
 
immerse”
 
or
 
“to
 
place
 
into.”
 
In
 
the
 
early
 
church,

was

the

(Acts

identification
 
with

For

on

27

the
 
inward

Spirit.
 - In Acts 10:44-48, the Gentiles received the Holy Spirit before they

ever
 
baptized

water,

water
 
baptism
 
was

 - Some of the earliest Christian writings (such as the Didache) 
mention
 
baptism,
 
but
 
its
 
focus
 
was
 
more
 
on
 
discipleship
 
and
 
teaching
 
rather

a
 
required
 
water

 Thus, the command in Matthew 28:19-20 refers to immersing new

into

truth
 
and

the
 
Father,
 
Son,
 
and

the

 The True Baptism: Baptism of the Holy Spirit Spiritual baptism places believers into Christ. 1 Corinthians 12:13 - “For by one Spirit are we all baptized into one body.” Romans 6:3 speaks of being baptized into Christ, referring to spiritual 
baptism.
 Galatians 3:27 emphasizes that being baptized into Christ is about faith, 
not
 
water
 
baptism.
 Paul emphasizes one baptism: Ephesians 4:5 - “One Lord, one faith, one baptism.” Acts 19:1-5 - Paul distinguishes between John’s baptism and the 
baptism
 
into
 
Christ.
 Problem Passages Explained 
28

Mark 16:15-16 - “He that believeth and is baptized shall be saved; 
but
 
he
 
that

not

damned.”

 John 3:5 - “Except a man be born of water and of the Spirit, he

talks
 
about

and

 Acts 2:38 - “Repent, and be baptized every one of you in the name

Christ

 Conclusion In the New Covenant, water baptism is not required for salvation. The

While

coming

Christ,

The

Christian
 
life.
 
As
 
believers,

shapes

and

 We Are New Creations in Christ (2 Corinthians 5:17) The moment we accepted Jesus Christ as Lord and Savior, we became new 
creations.
 
The
 
old
 
self
,

by

and

with

nature
 
that
 
is
 
aligned
 
with

will
 
and

new
 
creature:
 
old
 
things
 
are
 
passed
 
away;
 
behold,
 
all
 
things
 
are
 
become
 
new.”

by

old
 
identity
 
but
 
are

free
 
to
 
live
 
according

 - This new identity is not just a change in behavior, but a complete

of
 
our
 
spirit,
 
mind,
 
and
 
emotions,

in
 
our
 
union
 
with
 
Christ.
 We Are Created for Good Works (Ephesians 2:10) As believers, we are not only made new, but we are also created in Christ 
Jesus
 
for
 
good
 
works
.
 
Our
 
new
 
identity
 
comes
 
with
 
a
 
divine
 
purpose,
 
God
 
has
 
prepared

to

out

will
 
on
 
earth.

works
 
are
 
not

means
 
to
 
earn
 
salvation

unto

before

to

can
 
be
 
expressions

new
 
identity.
 Our New Identity Transforms How We Live Having a new identity in Christ is not just a theological concept, it has

implications
 
for
 
how

means:

in

 Who We Are in Christ - A Summary - We are new creations in Christ, free from the power of sin and

Conclusion In Christ, we are not the same as we once were. We have a new identity,

Believers' Class: WEEK 9 TOPIC: Pauline Prayers Introduction The Apostle Paul, in his epistles, recorded several powerful prayers that 
reveal
 
his
 
deep
 
concern
 
for
 
the
 
spiritual
 
growth
 
of
 
believers.
 
These
 
prayers
 
are
 
rich
 
in
 
scriptural
 
truth,
 
emphasizing
 
the
 
wisdom,
 
revelation,
 
strength,
 
love,
 
and
 
the
 
fullness
 
of
 
God.
 
Studying
 
and
 
applying
 
Pauline
 
prayers
 
can
 
transform
 
our
 
prayer
 
lives
 
and
 
strengthen
 
our
 
walk
 
with
 
God.
 Characteristics of Pauline Prayers 1. Christ-Centered Focus - Paul's prayers emphasize the supremacy 
and
 
work
 
of
 
Christ
 
in
 
the
 
believer’s
 
life.
 2. Spiritual Growth and Maturity - His prayers focus on wisdom, 
knowledge,
 
and
 
understanding
 
of
 
God's
 
will.
 3. Empowerment by the Holy Spirit - Paul intercedes for believers 
to
 
be
 
strengthened
 
with
 
divine
 
power.
 4. Thanksgiving and Praise - He often begins prayers by giving 
thanks
 
for
 
believers
 
and
 
acknowledging
 
God's
 
faithfulness.
 5. Intercession for the Church - Paul consistently prays for the 
corporate
 
body,
 
not
 
just
 
individuals.
 Key Pauline Prayers 1. Prayer for Wisdom and Revelation (Ephesians 1:15-23) Main Themes: - Spirit of wisdom and revelation ● Enlightenment of the heart ● Knowledge of God’s calling and power Paul prays that believers may know: ● The hope of God's calling ● The riches of His glorious inheritance ● The immeasurable power available to them 
43

2. Prayer for Strength and Love (Ephesians 3:14-21) Main Themes: ● Strengthened with might by the Spirit ● Christ dwelling in hearts through faith ● Rooted and grounded in love ● Experiencing the fullness of God Paul desires that believers be empowered to grasp the breadth, length, 
depth,
 
and
 
height
 
of
 
Christ’s
 
love,
 
leading
 
to
 
spiritual
 
fullness.
 3. Prayer for Discernment and Holiness (Philippians 1:9-11) Main Themes: ● Abounding love with knowledge and discernment ● Approving things that are excellent ● Being filled with the fruits of righteousness This prayer highlights the role of spiritual discernment in Christian 
maturity,
 
ensuring
 
believers
 
live
 
pure
 
and
 
blameless
 
lives.
 4. Prayer for Fruitfulness and Strength (Colossians 1:9-14) Main Themes: ● Filled with the knowledge of God's will ● Walking worthy of the Lord ● Strengthened with all might ● Giving thanks for redemption and inheritance Paul prays that believers walk in a manner pleasing to God , bearing fruit 
in
 
every
 
good
 
work
 
and
 
increasing
 
in
 
knowledge.
 5. Prayer for Encouragement and Establishment (2 Thessalonians 2:16-17, 
3:1-5)
 Main Themes: ● Comfort and strength in every good work ● Establishment in faith ● Direction into the love of God and patience of Christ 
44

This prayer emphasizes encouragement, stability, and steadfastness in 
the
 
midst
 
of
 
trials.
 Application of Pauline Prayers 1. Pray These Prayers Over Yourself and Others - Personalize 
them
 
by
 
inserting
 
names
 
or
 
situations.
 2. Align Your Prayer Life with Scriptural Priorities - Focus on 
spiritual
 
growth,
 
wisdom,
 
love,
 
and
 
empowerment
,
 
rather
 
than
 
just
 
material
 
needs.
 3. Use Pauline Prayers for Church Intercession - These prayers are 
powerful
 
tools
 
for
 
praying
 
for
 
our
 
church
 
community.
 4. Meditate on the Theological Truths - Each prayer contains 
profound
 
truths
 
about
 
God's
 
will,
 
Christ’s
 
work,
 
and
 
the
 
believer’s
 
position
 
in
 
Him.
 5. Develop a Habit of Thanksgiving - Paul’s prayers are often filled 
with
 
gratitude,
 
setting
 
an
 
example
 
for
 
us
 
to
 
follow.
 Conclusion Pauline prayers are a divine blueprint for effective prayer and spiritual 
growth.
 
They
 
shift
 
the
 
believer’s
 
focus
 
from
 
temporary
 
concerns
 
to
 
eternal
 
realities.
 
By
 
studying,
 
praying,
 
and
 
living
 
out
 
these
 
prayers,
 
we
 
align
 
ourselves
 
with
 
God’s
 
purposes
 
and
 
experience
 
the
 
transformative
 
power
 
of
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit
 
in
 
our
 
daily
 
lives.
 
45

in
 
it.
 1. The Local Church as God’s Design ● Jesus established the Church (Matthew 16:18) and called believers 
to
 
function
 
as
 
one
 
body
 
(1
 
Corinthians
 
12:12-27).
 ● The Church is described as the body of Christ, meaning every 
member
 
has
 
a
 
role
 
to
 
play
 
in
 
its
 
health
 
and
 
function.
 ● Believers are called to live in unity and grow together in faith 
(Ephesians
 
4:11-16).
 2. The Purpose of Fellowship in the Local Church Acts 2:42-47 provides a model of Christian fellowship. The early believers 
were
 
devoted
 
to:
 1. Teaching and Doctrine - Learning the Word of God and growing 
in
 
faith.
 2. Fellowship - Building strong relationships with other believers. 3. Breaking of Bread - Sharing in the Lord’s Supper and communal 
meals.
 4. Prayer - Strengthening their faith and dependence on God. Through these practices, they experienced spiritual growth, miracles, and 
unity.
 
46

3. Why We Need the Local Church a. Spiritual Growth and Maturity ● The local church provides teaching, correction, and encouragement 
(2
 
Timothy
 
3:16-17).
 ● Pastors and leaders help believers mature in their faith (Ephesians 
4:11-12).
 
 b. Fellowship and Support ● Christianity is not an individual journey; believers need one another 
(Ecclesiastes
 
4:9-12).
 ● The local church provides a community where we bear each other's 
burdens
 
(Galatians
 
6:2).
 c. Worship and Communion ● Corporate worship strengthens our faith (Psalm 22:3). ● Communion reminds us of Christ’s sacrifice and our unity as His 
body
 
(1
 
Corinthians
 
11:23-26).
 d. Accountability and Discipleship ● The church helps believers stay accountable in their walk with God 
(James
 
5:16).
 ● Through discipleship, believers are trained and equipped for 
ministry
 
(Matthew
 
28:19-20).
 e. Evangelism and Mission ● The Church is the vehicle for spreading the Gospel (Matthew 
28:19-20).
 ● Together, believers can make a greater impact in reaching the lost. 4. The Danger of Neglecting Church Fellowship Hebrews 10:25 warns against forsaking the assembly of believers. When 
we
 
isolate
 
ourselves,
 
we
 
risk:
 ● Spiritual stagnation - Lack of encouragement and growth. ● Vulnerability to sin - No accountability. ● Lack of support - Facing life’s struggles alone. 
 47

Application: How to Engage in the Local Church 1. Attend Regularly - Make church fellowship a priority in your life. 2. Serve Actively - Find a ministry to contribute to. 3. Build Relationships - Engage in small groups, prayer meetings, 
and
 
church
 
activities.
 4. Support the Church - Give your time, resources, and prayers to 
the
 
church’s
 
mission.
 5. Invite Others - Encourage others to be part of the fellowship. Conclusion The local church is an essential part of a believer’s life. It is a place of 
growth,
 
fellowship,
 
worship,
 
and
 
mission.
 
Just
 
as
 
the
 
early
 
church
 
thrived
 
in
 
unity,
 
devotion,
 
and
 
love,
 
every
 
believer
 
today
 
is
 
called
 
to
 
actively
 
participate
 
in
 
and
 
support
 
the
 
body
 
of
 
Christ.
 
By
 
committing
 
to
 
the
 
local
 
church,
 
we
 
align
 
ourselves
 
with
 
God’s
 
design
 
and
 
experience
 
the
 
fullness
 
of
 
Christian
 
life.
 
48

Believers' Class: WEEK 11 TOPIC: Handling Conflicts in the Church - Biblical Conflict 
Resolution
 
&
 
Extending
 
Grace
 
to
 
Others
 Introduction Conflict is inevitable in any community, including the church. As 
believers,
 
we
 
are
 
called
 
to
 
handle
 
disputes
 
in
 
a
 
manner
 
that
 
reflects
 
the
 
love,
 
humility,
 
and
 
wisdom
 
of
 
Christ.
 
The
 
Bible
 
provides
 
clear
 
principles
 
for
 
resolving
 
conflicts
 
and
 
extending
 
grace
 
to
 
others
 
when
 
they
 
fall.
 
This
 
lesson
 
will
 
explore
 
biblical
 
conflict
 
resolution,
 
forgiveness,
 
and
 
maintaining
 
unity
 
within
 
the
 
body
 
of
 
Christ.
 Understanding Conflict in the Church Conflict can arise due to various reasons, such as differences in opinion, 
misunderstandings,
 
pride,
 
or
 
even
 
sin.
 
The
 
goal
 
is
 
not
 
to
 
avoid
 
conflict
 
at
 
all
 
costs
 
but
 
to
 
handle
 
it
 
in
 
a
 
Christlike
 
way.
 Common Causes of Conflict in the Church: 1. Miscommunication - Failure to communicate clearly can lead to 
misunderstandings.
 2. Doctrinal Differences - Differing interpretations of Scripture may 
cause
 
division.
 3. Personality Clashes - Differences in background and temperament 
may
 
create
 
tension.
 4. Offenses and Sins - When believers wrong one another, conflict 
arises.
 5. Pride and Selfishness - An unwillingness to yield or consider 
others
 
breeds
 
division.
 
 Biblical Principles for Resolving Conflict The Bible offers a roadmap for handling conflict in a godly way: 1. Address Conflict with Love and Truth (Ephesians 4:15) 
49

● Speak the truth in love. ● Avoid gossip and slander. ● Confront with a heart of restoration, not condemnation. 
 2. Follow the Biblical Steps of Conflict Resolution (Matthew 18:15-17) Jesus outlined a clear process for resolving disputes: 1. Go privately to the person - Seek reconciliation one-on-one. 2. Take witnesses if needed - If unresolved, involve mature believers. 3. Bring it to the church - As a last resort, church leadership may 
intervene.
 4. Let them go if unrepentant - When reconciliation is impossible, 
maintain
 
love
 
but
 
establish
 
boundaries.
 
 3. Pursue Peace and Unity (Romans 12:18, Hebrews 12:14) ● Strive to live at peace with all. ● Avoid unnecessary quarrels and strife. ● Seek reconciliation over division. 
 4. Extend Forgiveness and Grace (Colossians 3:13, Matthew 6:14-15) ● Just as Christ forgave us, we must forgive others. ● True forgiveness means releasing resentment and seeking 
restoration.
 ● Forgiveness is not condoning sin but allowing God to heal hearts. 
 Having Grace When Others Fall In every church community, believers may stumble and fall. Our response 
should
 
reflect
 
the
 
grace
 
and
 
mercy
 
of
 
Christ.
 1. Restore Gently (Galatians 6:1-2) ● Approach fallen believers with humility. ● Help restore them without pride or condemnation. ● Bear one another’s burdens in love. 
 50

2. Avoid a Judgmental Attitude (James 4:11-12) ● We are called to correct with love, not to condemn. ● Recognize that we all need God’s grace daily. 
 3. Encourage Repentance and Accountability (2 Corinthians 7:10) ● Call for genuine repentance. ● Provide support and accountability to prevent future struggles. 
 4. Maintain Fellowship and Unity (1 Corinthians 1:10, John 17:21) ● A healthy church culture promotes forgiveness and reconciliation. ● Unity glorifies God and strengthens the church’s witness. 
 Conclusion Conflict in the church is an opportunity to grow in grace, humility, and 
love.
 
By
 
applying
 
biblical
 
principles,
 
seeking
 
peace,
 
and
 
extending
 
grace
 
to
 
others,
 
we
 
reflect
 
Christ’s
 
character
 
and
 
preserve
 
the
 
unity
 
of
 
the
 
body.
 
As
 
believers,
 
let
 
us
 
commit
 
to
 
handling
 
conflicts
 
with
 
wisdom,
 
forgiveness,
 
and
 
a
 
heart
 
for
 
reconciliation,
 
ensuring
 
that
 
our
 
fellowship
 
remains
 
a
 
testimony
 
of
 
God’s
 
love
 
to
 
the
 
world.
 
51

TERM 1: FOUNDATIONS OF THE FAITH (Weeks 1-15) Module 3: Christian Living (Weeks 12-15)

Believers' Class: WEEK 12 TOPIC: Victory Over Sin - Overcoming Temptation Key Scriptures: Romans 6:1-14, Galatians 5:16 Introduction As believers, we are called to live victoriously over sin. While temptation 
is
 
a
 
universal
 
experience,
 
God
 
has
 
given
 
us
 
the
 
power
 
to
 
overcome
 
it
 
through
 
the
 
work
 
of
 
Christ
 
and
 
the
 
indwelling
 
of
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit.
 
Understanding
 
our
 
position
 
in
 
Christ
 
and
 
walking
 
in
 
the
 
Spirit
 
are
 
essential
 
keys
 
to
 
overcoming
 
temptation
 
and
 
living
 
a
 
life
 
that
 
pleases
 
God.
 1. Understanding Sin and Temptation ● The Nature of Sin: Sin is rebellion against God and manifests in 
disobedience
 
to
 
His
 
will
 
(1
 
John
 
3:4).
 ● Temptation is Not Sin: Being tempted is not a sin, but yielding to 
it
 
leads
 
to
 
sin
 
(James
 
1:14-15).
 ● Common to All: No believer is exempt from temptation, but God 
provides
 
a
 
way
 
of
 
escape
 
(1
 
Corinthians
 
10:13).
 2. Our Victory Through Christ ● Dead to Sin, Alive in Christ (Romans 6:1-14): ○ Through Christ’s death and resurrection, we are no longer 
slaves
 
to
 
sin.
 ○ We are to reckon ourselves dead to sin but alive unto God. ○ Sin shall not have dominion over us because we are under 
grace.
 
 ● Crucifying the Flesh (Galatians 5:16-24): ○ Walking in the Spirit enables us to resist the desires of the 
flesh.
 ○ The works of the flesh are contrasted with the fruit of the 
Spirit.
 53

○ Those who belong to Christ have crucified the flesh with its 
passions
 
and
 
desires.
 3. Practical Steps to Overcome Temptation 1. Renew Your Mind with God’s Word (Romans 12:2): The Word 
of
 
God
 
transforms
 
our
 
thinking
 
and
 
helps
 
us
 
resist
 
sinful
 
desires.
 2. Be Led by the Spirit (Galatians 5:16): The Holy Spirit empowers 
us
 
to
 
walk
 
in
 
holiness.
 3. Avoid Triggers and Flee from Sin (2 Timothy 2:22): We must 
stay
 
away
 
from
 
situations
 
and
 
influences
 
that
 
lead
 
to
 
temptation.
 4. Pray for Strength (Matthew 26:41): Jesus taught that prayer 
keeps
 
us
 
from
 
falling
 
into
 
temptation.
 5. Use the Armor of God (Ephesians 6:10-18): Spiritual warfare 
requires
 
putting
 
on
 
the
 
full
 
armor
 
of
 
God
 
to
 
stand
 
against
 
the
 
enemy.
 6. Rely on God’s Grace (2 Corinthians 12:9): God’s grace 
strengthens
 
us
 
in
 
our
 
weaknesses.
 7. Surround Yourself with Godly Influence (Hebrews 10:24-25): 
Fellowship
 
with
 
believers
 
strengthens
 
our
 
faith
 
and
 
accountability.
 4. The Power of Confession and Restoration ● If a believer falls into sin, confession and repentance restore 
fellowship
 
with
 
God
 
(1
 
John
 
1:9).
 ● God’s grace is sufficient to restore and strengthen us for continued 
victory.
 
 Conclusion Victory over sin is not about striving in our own strength but relying on the 
finished
 
work
 
of
 
Christ
 
and
 
the
 
power
 
of
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit.
 
As
 
we
 
renew
 
our
 
minds,
 
walk
 
in
 
the
 
Spirit,
 
and
 
apply
 
God’s
 
Word,
 
we
 
will
 
overcome
 
temptation
 
and
 
live
 
a
 
life
 
that
 
glorifies
 
God.
 
54

Reflection Questions: 1. What are some areas where you struggle with temptation? 2. How can you apply the principles from this lesson to gain victory? 3. What scriptures can you meditate on to strengthen your resolve 
against
 
sin?
 
 Memory Verse: Romans 6:14 - "For sin shall not have dominion over 
you:
 
for
 
ye
 
are
 
not
 
under
 
the
 
law,
 
but
 
under
 
grace."
 
55

constantly

God's
 
purposes
 
in
 
our
 
lives.
 
However,
 
God
 
has
 
equipped
 
us
 
with
 
everything
 
we
 
need
 
to
 
stand
 
firm
 
and

Ephesians
 
6:10-18
 
outlines
 
the

to

and

in

 3. Exercising Our Authority in Christ ● Know Your Identity in Christ - We are seated with Christ in

(Ephesians

(1
 
Peter
 
5:8-9).
 
 4. Practical Steps for Victory ● Daily Devotion - Spend time in prayer and the Word to strengthen

 ● Guard Your Thoughts - Take every thought captive (2 Corinthians

 ● Speak Life - Use your words to declare God’s promises over your

 ● Stay in Fellowship - Surround yourself with believers who will

and

● Engage in Worship - Worship is a powerful weapon against

attacks.
 
 Conclusion Victory in spiritual warfare comes through faith, obedience, and reliance

God’s
 
power.
 
By

and

(Romans

Believers' Class: WEEK 14 TOPIC: Stewardship of Resources: Tithes, Offerings, and Giving A Biblical Perspective on New Covenant Giving Introduction Stewardship in the kingdom of God is a fundamental principle that reflects 
our
 
acknowledgment
 
of
 
God’s
 
ownership
 
over
 
all
 
things.
 
As
 
believers,
 
we
 
are
 
called
 
to
 
manage
 
our
 
resources
 
in
 
a
 
way
 
that
 
honors
 
God,
 
serves
 
the
 
church,
 
and
 
meets
 
the
 
needs
 
of
 
others.
 
While
 
the
 
Old
 
Testament
 
provided
 
clear
 
instructions
 
on
 
tithing,
 
the
 
New
 
Testament
 
does
 
not
 
mandate
 
a
 
fixed
 
percentage
 
but
 
emphasizes
 
the
 
heart,
 
attitude,
 
and
 
purpose
 
of
 
giving.
 This manual explores the biblical teaching on tithes, offerings, and giving, 
focusing
 
on
 
how
 
believers
 
should
 
steward
 
their
 
resources
 
under
 
the
 
New
 
Covenant.
 1. The Principle of Stewardship A. God Owns Everything “The earth is the Lord’s, and the fulness thereof; the world, and 
they
 
that
 
dwell
 
therein.”
 
(Psalm
 
24:1)
 “For all things come of thee, and of thine own have we given thee.” 
(1
 
Chronicles
 
29:14b)
 Believers are not owners but stewards of the resources God has entrusted 
to
 
them.
 
Stewardship
 
requires
 
faithfulness,
 
accountability,
 
and
 
the
 
recognition
 
that
 
everything
 
we
 
have
 
comes
 
from
 
God.
 B. The Purpose of Wealth and Resources • To meet personal and family needs (1 Timothy 5:8) • To support the work of the ministry (1 Corinthians 9:13-14) • To help those in need (Galatians 6:10, Acts 4:34-35) 
59

2. Tithing in the Old Testament vs. Giving in the New Covenant A. The Old Testament Command on Tithing “And all the tithe of the land, whether of the seed of the land, or of 
the
 
fruit
 
of
 
the
 
tree,
 
is
 
the
 
Lord’s:
 
it
 
is
 
holy
 
unto
 
the
 
Lord.”
 
(Leviticus
 
27:30)
 “Bring ye all the tithes into the storehouse, that there may be meat 
in
 
mine
 
house,
 
and
 
prove
 
me
 
now
 
herewith,
 
saith
 
the
 
Lord
 
of
 
hosts,
 
if
 
I
 
will
 
not
 
open
 
you
 
the
 
windows
 
of
 
heaven,
 
and
 
pour
 
you
 
out
 
a
 
blessing,
 
that
 
there
 
shall
 
not
 
be
 
room
 
enough
 
to
 
receive
 
it.”
 
(Malachi
 
3:10)
 In the Old Testament, tithing was part of the Mosaic Law, requiring 
Israelites
 
to
 
give
 
a
 
tenth
 
of
 
their
 
produce,
 
livestock,
 
and
 
income
 
to
 
support
 
the
 
Levites,
 
the
 
temple,
 
and
 
the
 
needy.
 B. The New Testament Perspective on Giving 1. Giving is Commanded, But Amount is Not Mandated The New Testament does not command believers to give a specific 
percentage.
 
Instead,
 
it
 
emphasizes
 
giving
 
as
 
an
 
act
 
of
 
worship
 
and
 
gratitude.
 “Every man according as he purposeth in his heart, so let him give; 
not
 
grudgingly,
 
or
 
of
 
necessity:
 
for
 
God
 
loveth
 
a
 
cheerful
 
giver.”
 
(2
 
Corinthians
 
9:7)
 2. Giving is a Response to God’s Blessing Giving is an acknowledgment that God has blessed us. It is a natural 
response
 
to
 
His
 
provision.
 “Upon the first day of the week let every one of you lay by him in 
store,
 
as
 
God
 
hath
 
prospered
 
him,
 
that
 
there
 
be
 
no
 
gatherings
 
when
 
I
 
come.”
 
(1
 
Corinthians
 
16:2)
 
60

3. Sacrificial and Generous Giving is Encouraged “But this I say, He which soweth sparingly shall reap also 
sparingly;
 
and
 
he
 
which
 
soweth
 
bountifully
 
shall
 
reap
 
also
 
bountifully.”
 
(2
 
Corinthians
 
9:6)
 “They first gave their own selves to the Lord, and unto us by the 
will
 
of
 
God.”
 
(2
 
Corinthians
 
8:5)
 4. Needs in the Church Must be Met The early church responded to needs in the body of Christ through 
generous
 
giving.
 “Neither was there any among them that lacked: for as many as 
were
 
possessors
 
of
 
lands
 
or
 
houses
 
sold
 
them,
 
and
 
brought
 
the
 
prices
 
of
 
the
 
things
 
that
 
were
 
sold,
 
and
 
laid
 
them
 
down
 
at
 
the
 
apostles’
 
feet:
 
and
 
distribution
 
was
 
made
 
unto
 
every
 
man
 
according
 
as
 
he
 
had
 
need.”
 
(Acts
 
4:34-35)
 3. Offerings and Special Giving in the Church A. Freewill Offerings Offerings in the church should be freewill, from the heart, and not coerced. “Let him give; not grudgingly, or of necessity.” (2 Corinthians 9:7) B. Giving to Church Needs There are moments when specific needs arise in the church, and believers 
are
 
encouraged
 
to
 
meet
 
those
 
needs.
 “And let us consider one another to provoke unto love and to good 
works.”
 
(Hebrews
 
10:24)
 C. Giving to the Poor and Missions Helping those in need is a crucial part of biblical giving. 
61

“He that hath pity upon the poor lendeth unto the Lord; and that 
which
 
he
 
hath
 
given
 
will
 
he
 
pay
 
him
 
again.”
 
(Proverbs
 
19:17)
 4. The Heart and Attitude of Giving A. Giving Should Be Cheerful “God loveth a cheerful giver.” (2 Corinthians 9:7) B. Giving Should Be Generous “Freely ye have received, freely give.” (Matthew 10:8) C. Giving Should Be Done in Faith “Honour the Lord with thy substance, and with the firstfruits of all 
thine
 
increase.”
 
(Proverbs
 
3:9)
 D. Giving Should Not Be Manipulated The New Testament does not teach forced giving but emphasizes 
voluntary
 
generosity.
 “That thy alms may be in secret: and thy Father which seeth in 
secret
 
himself
 
shall
 
reward
 
thee
 
openly.”
 
(Matthew
 
6:4)
 5. The Blessing of Giving 1. God Supplies the Giver’s Needs “But my God shall supply all your need according to his riches in glory by 
Christ
 
Jesus.”
 
(Philippians
 
4:19)
 2. Giving Opens Doors for More Grace “Give, and it shall be given unto you; good measure, pressed down, and 
shaken
 
together,
 
and
 
running
 
over,
 
shall
 
men
 
give
 
into
 
your
 
bosom.”
 
(Luke
 
6:38)
 3. Giving Strengthens the Church and Expands the Gospel “Not because I desire a gift: but I desire fruit that may abound to your 
account.”
 
(Philippians
 
4:17)
 
62

Conclusion: Living as Faithful Stewards Stewardship in the New Testament is not about percentages but about 
faithfulness,
 
gratitude,
 
and
 
obedience
 
to
 
the
 
leading
 
of
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit.
 
Giving
 
is
 
a
 
response
 
to
 
God’s
 
blessings,
 
a
 
way
 
to
 
support
 
the
 
work
 
of
 
the
 
ministry,
 
and
 
an
 
expression
 
of
 
love
 
for
 
others.
 
As
 
believers,
 
we
 
must
 
give
 
cheerfully,
 
sacrificially,
 
and
 
with
 
the
 
right
 
heart,
 
trusting
 
that
 
God
 
will
 
always
 
provide
 
for
 
our
 
needs.
 May we be found faithful stewards of all that God has entrusted to us. 
Amen.
 
63

Believers' Class: WEEK 15 TOPIC: Living as a Witness - Evangelism and Discipleship Key Scriptures: Matthew 28:19-20, Romans 1:16 Introduction As believers, we are called not only to live out our faith but also to share it 
with
 
others.
 
Evangelism
 
and
 
discipleship
 
are
 
fundamental
 
aspects
 
of
 
the
 
Great
 
Commission
 
given
 
by
 
Jesus.
 
We
 
are
 
witnesses
 
of
 
Christ's
 
love
 
and
 
saving
 
power,
 
and
 
we
 
are
 
charged
 
with
 
spreading
 
the
 
gospel
 
to
 
the
 
world.
 1. Understanding Evangelism Evangelism is the act of proclaiming the good news of Jesus Christ. It is 
not
 
reserved
 
for
 
pastors
 
or
 
missionaries
 
alone;
 
every
 
believer
 
is
 
called
 
to
 
share
 
their
 
faith.
 A. The Mandate for Evangelism Matthew 28:19-20 - Jesus commands us to go and make 
disciples
 
of
 
all
 
nations.
 Romans 1:16 - The gospel is the power of God unto 
salvation
 
for
 
everyone
 
who
 
believes.
 Acts 1:8 - We are empowered by the Holy Spirit to be 
witnesses.
 
 B. The Message of Evangelism ● God loves the world and desires that none perish (John 3:16, 2 
Peter
 
3:9).
 ● Sin separates humanity from God (Romans 3:23, Isaiah 59:2). ● Jesus is the only way to salvation (John 14:6, Acts 4:12). ● Salvation is received by grace through faith (Ephesians 2:8-9, 
Romans
 
10:9-10).
 
 
64

C. The Methods of Evangelism ● Personal Testimony: Sharing your story of salvation (John 4:29, 
Revelation
 
12:11).
 ● Lifestyle Evangelism: Living in a way that reflects Christ 
(Matthew
 
5:16,
 
1
 
Peter
 
3:15).
 ● Direct Evangelism: Engaging in conversations, preaching, and 
distributing
 
gospel
 
materials.
 ● Media and Technology: Utilizing social media, podcasts, and 
videos
 
to
 
spread
 
the
 
gospel.
 
 2. The Call to Discipleship Evangelism does not end at conversion; new believers must be nurtured in 
their
 
faith.
 
Discipleship
 
is
 
the
 
process
 
of
 
helping
 
others
 
grow
 
in
 
their
 
relationship
 
with
 
Christ.
 A. The Importance of Discipleship ● Jesus modeled discipleship by training His followers (Mark 3:14, 
Luke
 
6:40).
 ● The early church was devoted to learning and fellowship (Acts 
2:42).
 ● Mature believers are called to teach and equip others (2 Timothy 
2:2,
 
Hebrews
 
5:12-14).
 
 B. The Process of Discipleship ● Teaching Sound Doctrine: Helping believers understand the Word 
(2
 
Timothy
 
3:16-17).
 ● Encouraging Spiritual Growth: Prayer, fasting, and studying 
Scripture
 
(Colossians
 
2:6-7).
 ● Providing Accountability: Building relationships that foster 
growth
 
(Hebrews
 
10:24-25).
 ● Training for Ministry: Encouraging service and leadership in the 
church
 
(Ephesians
 
4:11-12).
 
 65

3. Overcoming Barriers to Evangelism and Discipleship Many believers struggle with fear, inadequacy, or lack of knowledge when 
it
 
comes
 
to
 
sharing
 
their
 
faith
 
and
 
discipling
 
others.
 
Here
 
are
 
ways
 
to
 
overcome
 
these
 
challenges:
 ● Fear of Rejection: Remember that salvation is God’s work (John 
6:44,
 
1
 
Corinthians
 
3:6-7).

Believers' Class: WEEK 16 TOPIC: The Origin and Authority of the Bible 
 
How
 
the
 
Bible
 
Came
 
to
 
Be
 
 Key Scriptures: 2 Timothy 3:16-17, 2 Peter 1:20-21 Introduction The Bible is the inspired Word of God and the final authority for faith and 
practice.
 
It
 
is
 
unique
 
among
 
all
 
writings
 
in
 
its
 
divine
 
origin,
 
historical
 
reliability,
 
and

power.

how
 
the
 
Bible
 
came
 
to
 
be
 
helps
 
us
 
appreciate
 
its
 
authority
 
and
 
trustworthiness
 
in
 
our
 
lives
 
as
 
believers.
 1. The Divine Inspiration of Scripture (2 Timothy 3:16-17) - “All scripture is given by inspiration of God, 
and
 
is
 
profitable
 
for
 
doctrine,
 
for
 
reproof,
 
for
 
correction,
 
for
 
instruction
 
in
 
righteousness:
 
That
 
the
 
man
 
of
 
God
 
may
 
be
 
perfect,
 
thoroughly
 
furnished
 
unto
 
all
 
good
 
works.”
 ● The term “inspiration” means “God-breathed” (Greek: 
theopneustos
),
 
signifying
 
that
 
the
 
Bible
 
is
 
not
 
merely
 
human
 
literature
 
but
 
divinely
 
revealed.
 ● Every part of Scripture is inspired, from Genesis to Revelation, and 
serves
 
a
 
purpose
 
in
 
teaching,
 
correcting,
 
and
 
equipping
 
believers.
 
 (2 Peter 1:20-21) - “Knowing this first, that no prophecy of the 
scripture
 
is
 
of
 
any
 
private
 
interpretation.
 
For
 
the
 
prophecy
 
came
 
not
 
in
 
old
 
time
 
by
 
the
 
will
 
of
 
man:
 
but
 
holy
 
men
 
of
 
God
 
spake
 
as
 
they
 
were
 
moved
 
by
 
the
 
Holy
 
Ghost.”
 
69

● The Bible was written by human authors, but they were moved by 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit,
 
ensuring
 
the
 
message
 
was
 
God’s
 
and
 
not
 
their
 
own
 
opinions.
 ● This process is known as verbal plenary inspiration, meaning every 
word
 
and
 
every
 
part
 
of
 
Scripture
 
is
 
fully
 
inspired
 
by
 
God.
 
 2. The Formation of the Bible The Bible consists of 66 books (39 in the Old Testament, 27 in the New 
Testament),
 
written
 
over
 
a
 
period
 
of
 
about
 
1,500
 
years
 
by
 
more
 
than
 
40
 
authors
 
from
 
different
 
backgrounds
 
and
 
time
 
periods,
 
yet
 
maintaining
 
perfect
 
unity.
 A. The Old Testament ● The Old Testament was written in Hebrew (with some portions in 
Aramaic)
 
and
 
was
 
recognized
 
as
 
Scripture
 
by
 
the
 
Jewish
 
people.
 ● Jesus and the apostles affirmed the Old Testament as the 
authoritative
 
Word
 
of
 
God
 
(Luke
 
24:27,
 
44).
 ● It includes the Law (Torah), Prophets, and Writings, all pointing to 
the
 
coming
 
Messiah.
 
 B. The New Testament ● The New Testament was written in Greek and consists of Gospels, 
Epistles,
 
and
 
Revelation.
 ● The early church recognized the writings of the apostles as divinely 
inspired
 
and
 
authoritative.
 ● By the 4th century, church councils affirmed the 27 books of the 
New
 
Testament
 
as
 
Scripture,
 
though
 
they
 
were
 
already
 
widely
 
accepted
 
by
 
the
 
early
 
believers.
 
 3. The Authority of the Bible 
70

The Bible’s authority rests on its divine authorship, its historical accuracy, 
and
 
its
 
spiritual
 
power.
 A. The Bible Is Infallible and Inerrant ● Infallible means it is completely trustworthy in all matters of faith 
and
 
practice.
 ● Inerrant means it is without error in its original manuscripts. 
 B. The Bible Is the Final Authority ● It supersedes human traditions, opinions, and experiences (Isaiah 
40:8,
 
Matthew
 
24:35).
 ● It is the standard by which all teachings and doctrines must be 
tested
 
(Acts
 
17:11).
 ● It provides guidance for Christian living, equipping believers for 
every
 
good
 
work
 
(Psalm
 
119:105).
 
 C. The Bible’s Power and Preservation ● The Bible has survived countless attempts to be destroyed or 
discredited
 
throughout
 
history
 
(e.g.,

under

emperors,
 
skepticism
 
in
 
modern
 
times),
 
yet
 
it
 
remains
 
the
 
most
 
widely
 
read
 
and
 
distributed
 
book
 
in
 
the
 
world.
 ● God has preserved His Word so that His truth remains available for 
every
 
generation
 
(Matthew
 
5:18).
 
 4. How We Should Respond to the Bible ● Read and Study It Regularly - Joshua 1:8 commands us to meditate 
on
 
God’s
 
Word
 
day
 
and
 
night.
 ● Apply It in Daily Life - James 1:22 exhorts believers to be doers of 
the
 
Word,
 
not
 
just
 
hearers.
 ● Defend It with Boldness - 1 Peter 3:15 urges us to always be ready 
to
 
give
 
an
 
answer
 
for
 
our
 
faith.
 
71

● Trust in Its Power - Hebrews 4:12 describes the Word as living and 
active,
 
able
 
to
 
transform
 
hearts
 
and
 
minds.
 
 Conclusion The Bible is not just a religious book; it is God’s revelation to mankind. 
Understanding
 
its
 
divine
 
origin
 
and
 
authority
 
strengthens
 
our
 
confidence
 
in
 
its
 
teachings
 
and
 
our
 
commitment
 
to

by
 
its
 
truths.
 
As
 
believers,
 
we
 
must
 
cherish,
 
study,
 
and
 
proclaim
 
the
 
Word
 
of
 
God,
 
knowing
 
that
 
it
 
is
 
the
 
foundation
 
of
 
our
 
faith
 
and
 
the
 
key
 
to
 
spiritual
 
growth.

Believers' Class: Week 17 TOPIC: Bible Structure and Canonization Introduction The Bible is the inspired Word of God, consisting of two major divisions: 
the
 
Old
 
Testament
 
and
 
the
 
New
 
Testament.
 
Understanding
 
how
 
these
 
books
 
were
 
structured
 
and
 
selected
 
into
 
the
 
biblical
 
canon
 
helps
 
believers
 
appreciate
 
the
 
authority
 
and
 
reliability
 
of
 
Scripture.
 1. The Structure of the Bible The Bible consists of 66 books divided into two main sections: A. The Old Testament (39 Books) The Old Testament was primarily written in Hebrew (with small portions 
in
 
Aramaic)
 
and
 
contains
 
books
 
that
 
were
 
recognized
 
as
 
Scripture
 
by
 
the
 
Jewish
 
people
 
before
 
the
 
coming
 
of
 
Christ.
 
It
 
is
 
traditionally
 
divided
 
into:
 1. The Law (Torah/Pentateuch) - 5 Books ○ Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, Deuteronomy 2. The Historical Books - 12 Books ○ Joshua, Judges, Ruth, 1 & 2 Samuel, 1 & 2 Kings, 1 & 2 
Chronicles,
 
Ezra,
 
Nehemiah,
 
Esther
 3. The Poetic and Wisdom Books - 5 Books ○ Job, Psalms, Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, Song of Solomon 4. The Major Prophets - 5 Books ○ Isaiah, Jeremiah, Lamentations, Ezekiel, Daniel 5. The Minor Prophets - 12 Books ○ Hosea, Joel, Amos, Obadiah, Jonah, Micah, Nahum, 
Habakkuk,
 
Zephaniah,
 
Haggai,
 
Zechariah,
 
Malachi
 
 B. The New Testament (27 Books) Written in Greek, the New Testament contains the revelation of Jesus 
Christ
 
and
 
the
 
foundation
 
of
 
Christian
 
doctrine.
 
It
 
is
 
divided
 
into:
 
73

1. The Gospels - 4 Books ○ Matthew, Mark, Luke, John 2. The Historical Book - 1 Book ○ Acts 3. The Pauline Epistles (Letters) - 13 Books ○ Romans, 1 & 2 Corinthians, Galatians, Ephesians, 
Philippians,
 
Colossians,
 
1
 
&
 
2
 
Thessalonians,
 
1
 
&
 
2
 
Timothy,
 
Titus,
 
Philemon
 4. The General Epistles - 8 Books ○ Hebrews, James, 1 & 2 Peter, 1, 2, & 3 John, Jude 5. The Prophetic Book - 1 Book ○ Revelation 
 2. The Canonization of Scripture The term "canon" means a rule or standard. The biblical canon refers to 
the
 
collection
 
of
 
books
 
recognized
 
as
 
inspired
 
by
 
God
 
and
 
authoritative
 
for
 
faith
 
and
 
practice.
 A. Canonization of the Old Testament ● The Jewish community recognized the 39 books of the Old 
Testament
 
as
 
authoritative
 
long
 
before
 
the
 
time
 
of
 
Christ.
 ● Jesus and the apostles referred to these writings as Scripture (Luke 
24:44,
 
Matthew
 
5:17-18).
 ● The Council of Jamnia (AD 90) reaffirmed the Old Testament 
books
 
but
 
did
 
not
 
introduce
 
new
 
ones.
 
 B. Canonization of the New Testament The early church carefully evaluated writings before recognizing them as 
Scripture.
 
The
 
criteria
 
used
 
included:
 1. Apostolic Authority - Was the book written by an apostle or a 
close
 
associate
 
of
 
an
 
apostle?
 
74

2. Orthodoxy (Agreement with Christian Doctrine) - Did the book 
align
 
with
 
the
 
teachings
 
of
 
Jesus
 
and
 
the
 
apostles?
 3. Widespread Usage - Was the book widely accepted and used by 
the
 
early
 
church?
 4. Divine Inspiration - Did the book exhibit evidence of being 
inspired
 
by
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit?
 
 By AD 367 , Athanasius (Bishop of Alexandria) listed the 27 New 
Testament
 
books
 
as
 
Scripture,
 
which
 
was
 
later
 
affirmed
 
by
 
the
 
Councils
 
of
 
Hippo
 
(AD
 
393)
 
and
 
Carthage
 
(AD
 
397).
 3. The Apocrypha and Deuterocanonical Books The Apocrypha refers to a collection of Jewish writings included in some 
versions
 
of
 
the
 
Old
 
Testament
 
(e.g.,
 
the
 
Roman
 
Catholic
 
Bible)
 
but
 
not
 
in
 
the
 
Hebrew
 
Bible
 
or
 
Protestant
 
Bible.
 
These
 
books
 
were
 
written
 
between
 
the
 
Old
 
and
 
New
 
Testament
 
periods
 
but
 
were
 
not
 
universally
 
accepted
 
as
 
inspired
 
Scripture.
 Reasons why the Apocrypha is not included in the Protestant Bible: ● The Jewish community did not recognize it as Scripture. ● Jesus and the apostles never quoted from it. ● The early church fathers had differing opinions about its authority. ● It contains historical and doctrinal inconsistencies. 
 4. The Authority of the Bible The Bible is authoritative because it is the inspired Word of God: 2 Timothy 3:16-17 - "All scripture is given by inspiration of God, 
and
 
is
 
profitable
 
for
 
doctrine,
 
for
 
reproof,
 
for
 
correction,
 
for
 
instruction
 
in
 
righteousness."
 
 2 Peter 1:20-21 - "Knowing this first, that no prophecy of the 
scripture
 
is
 
of
 
any
 
private
 
interpretation.
 
For
 
the
 
prophecy
 
came
 
not
 
75

in old time by the will of man: but holy men of God spake as they 
were
 
moved
 
by
 
the
 
Holy
 
Ghost."
 
 5. Why Understanding the Canon is Important ● It Strengthens Our Faith - Knowing the Bible’s structure and 
history
 
affirms
 
its
 
reliability.
 ● It Guards Against False Teachings - Understanding how books 
were
 
selected
 
helps
 
distinguish
 
Scripture
 
from
 
other
 
religious
 
writings.
 ● It Equips Us for Ministry - Recognizing the authority of God’s 
Word
 
enables
 
us
 
to
 
confidently
 
share
 
and
 
defend
 
the
 
faith.
 
 Conclusion The Bible is not a random collection of writings but a divinely inspired and 
carefully
 
preserved
 
revelation
 
of
 
God.
 
Understanding
 
its
 
structure
 
and
 
the
 
process
 
of
 
canonization
 
assures
 
us
 
of
 
its
 
authority
 
and
 
reliability.
 
As
 
believers,
 
we
 
must
 
study,
 
meditate
 
on,
 
and
 
apply
 
the
 
Scriptures
 
in
 
our
 
daily
 
lives,
 
knowing
 
that
 
they
 
are
 
the
 
ultimate
 
guide
 
for
 
faith
 
and
 
practice.
 Reflection Questions 1. Why is it important to understand how the books of the Bible were 
selected?
 2. How does knowing the structure of the Bible help you in your study 
of
 
Scripture?
 3. How can you grow in your confidence in the authority of the Bible?

for
 
doctrine,
 
for
 
reproof,
 
for
 
correction,
 
for
 
instruction
 
in
 
righteousness."
 
-
 
2
 
Timothy
 
3:16

The Commandments and the Law The Law given through Moses was meant to: ● Define God’s standard of righteousness (Leviticus 19:2). ● Show Israel how to live as a holy nation (Deuteronomy 6:4-5). ● Establish moral, ceremonial, and civil laws to govern the people. ● Point toward the need for redemption , ultimately fulfilled in

 Overview of the Historical Books The Historical Books include twelve books that document Israel's national

mercy,
 
and

(Judges
 
2:11-23).

29:10,

1:1-4).

enduring
 
faithfulness.

(Nehemiah
 
2:4-5).

directs
 
our
 
lives.

and
 
protection.

things,

in
 
Nehemiah’s
 
leadership.
 5. God Uses Ordinary People - No matter your background, God has 
a
 
role
 
for
 
you
 
in
 
His
 
plan.
 Conclusion The Historical Books provide valuable lessons about God’s dealings with 
His
 
people.
 
They
 
remind
 
us
 
of
 
His
 
justice,
 
mercy,
 
and
 
faithfulness.
 
By

books,

with
