and
 
deepen
 
their
 
worship
 
life.
 Psalms Reading Plan ● Read five chapters of Psalms each day. ● Add 30 to the previous number to stay on track. Example: ○ Day 1: Psalms 1, 31, 61, 91, 121 ○ Day 2: Psalms 2, 32, 62, 92, 122 ○ Day 3: Psalms 3, 33, 63, 93, 123 ○ Continue this pattern until you complete all 150 Psalms in 
30
 
days.
 
 Proverbs Reading Plan ● Read one chapter of Proverbs per day. ● Since Proverbs has 31 chapters, reading one chapter daily allows 
you
 
to
 
finish
 
it
 
in
 
a
 
month.
 85

● This ensures daily exposure to godly wisdom applicable to life’s 
situations.
 
 Assignment 1. Follow the Psalms and Proverbs reading plan for the next 30 
days.
 2. Write down five key lessons from Proverbs that you can apply to 
your
 
life.
 3. Watch the teaching series on Song of Solomon on our YouTube page: Great Haven Ministries 4. Journal your reflections on a Psalm or Proverb that speaks to your 
current
 
season
 
of
 
life.
 
 Conclusion The Wisdom and Poetic Books serve as a guide for worship, wisdom, and 
reflection
 
on
 
life’s
 
purpose.
 
As
 
we
 
engage
 
with
 
Psalms
 
in
 
worship,
 
Proverbs
 
in
 
practical
 
living,
 
and
 
Ecclesiastes
 
in
 
reflection,
 
we
 
grow
 
in
 
understanding
 
our
 
relationship
 
with
 
God.
 
May
 
this
 
study
 
encourage
 
you
 
to
 
cultivate
 
a
 
lifestyle
 
of
 
devotion,
 
wisdom,
 
and
 
worship
 
in
 
Christ!
86

Believers' Class: Week 21 TOPIC: The Major & Minor Prophets - Isaiah to Malachi (Prophecy, 
Messianic
 
Promises)
 Introduction The prophetic books of the Bible serve as God's voice to His people, 
calling
 
them
 
to
 
repentance,
 
warning
 
of
 
judgment,
 
and
 
proclaiming
 
the
 
coming
 
of
 
the
 
Messiah.
 
These
 
books
 
are
 
traditionally
 
divided
 
into
 
Major
 
Prophets
 
and
 
Minor
 
Prophets
,
 
not
 
because
 
of
 
their
 
importance
 
but
 
because
 
of
 
their
 
length
 
and
 
scope.
 
While
 
the
 
Major
 
Prophets
 
contain
 
more
 
extensive
 
writings,
 
the
 
Minor
 
Prophets
 
still
 
hold
 
powerful
 
and
 
profound
 
messages
 
from
 
God.
 Understanding the Prophetic Books The prophetic books (Isaiah to Malachi) cover several centuries of Israel's 
history,
 
from
 
the
 
time
 
of
 
the
 
divided
 
kingdom
 
to
 
the
 
post-exilic
 
period.
 
They
 
were
 
written
 
by
 
prophets
 
whom
 
God
 
raised
 
up
 
to
 
speak
 
on
 
His
 
behalf.
 
These
 
books
 
contain
 
three
 
key
 
themes
:
 1. Judgment - Prophets warned of impending judgment due to 
Israel’s
 
sin
 
and
 
unfaithfulness.
 2. Hope & Restoration - They pointed toward God's mercy, the 
coming
 
Messiah,
 
and
 
the
 
restoration
 
of
 
His
 
people.
 3. Messianic Prophecy - Many of their prophecies found fulfillment 
in
 
Jesus
 
Christ.
 
 The Major Prophets The Major Prophets include Isaiah, Jeremiah, Lamentations, Ezekiel, 
and
 
Daniel
.
 
These
 
books
 
are
 
longer
 
and
 
contain
 
extensive
 
visions,
 
prophecies,
 
and
 
theological
 
insights.
 1. Isaiah (66 Chapters) - The Miniature Bible ● Often called the "Fifth Gospel" because of its strong Messianic 
focus.
 
87

● Isaiah has 66 chapters , mirroring the 66 books of the Bible: the 
first
 
39
 
chapters
 
align
 
with
 
the
 
Old
 
Testament’s
 
themes
 
of
 
judgment,
 
while
 
the
 
last
 
27
 
chapters
 
reflect
 
New
 
Testament
 
themes
 
of
 
grace
 
and
 
salvation.
 ● Isaiah 53 is one of the clearest prophecies about Jesus’ suffering 
and
 
redemptive
 
work.
 
 2. Jeremiah (52 Chapters) - The Weeping Prophet ● Known for his deep sorrow over Israel's rebellion and impending 
exile.
 ● Introduced the concept of the New Covenant (Jeremiah 31:31-34), 
which
 
was
 
fulfilled
 
in
 
Christ.
 ● He prophesied Israel’s captivity in Babylon for 70 years (Jeremiah 
25:11-12).
 
 3. Lamentations (5 Chapters) - A Cry of Mourning ● Written by Jeremiah as a poetic lament over Jerusalem’s 
destruction.
 ● Each chapter is an acrostic poem except the final chapter. ● Lamentations 3:22-23 speaks of God’s mercies being new every 
morning
.
 
 4. Ezekiel (48 Chapters) - The Prophet of Visions ● Had incredible visions (e.g., the valley of dry bones in Ezekiel 37). ● Introduced the concept of a new heart and new spirit (Ezekiel 
36:26-27).
 ● He saw God’s glory depart from the temple due to Israel’s sin and 
later
 
promised
 
its
 
return.
 
 5. Daniel (12 Chapters) - The Prophet of Kingdoms ● Contains apocalyptic visions and narratives about faithful living in 
exile.
 
88

● Prophesied about world empires and the coming of the Messianic 
Kingdom
 
(Daniel
 
2,
 
7,
 
9).
 ● The 70 weeks prophecy (Daniel 9:24-27) foretells Christ’s coming 
and
 
the
 
end
 
times.
 
 The Minor Prophets The 12 Minor Prophets are called "minor" because their books are 
shorter,
 
not
 
because
 
they
 
are
 
less
 
significant.
 
Their
 
messages
 
are
 
equally
 
powerful
 
and
 
essential
 
to
 
biblical
 
prophecy.
 1. Hosea - The Prophet of God’s Love ● Hosea’s life mirrors God’s faithful love to an unfaithful Israel. ● Hosea 6:6: "For I desire mercy, not sacrifice." 
 2. Joel - The Prophet of the Day of the Lord ● Prophesied about Pentecost (Joel 2:28-29, fulfilled in Acts 2). 
 3. Amos - The Prophet of Justice ● Strongly condemned social injustice and religious hypocrisy. 
 4. Obadiah - The Prophet Against Edom ● The shortest Old Testament book, pronouncing judgment on Edom. 
 5. Jonah - The Reluctant Prophet ● A story of God’s mercy to Nineveh and a lesson in obedience. ● Jesus referenced Jonah as a sign of His resurrection (Matthew 
12:40).
 
 6. Micah - The Prophet of the Messiah’s Birth ● Prophesied that Christ would be born in Bethlehem (Micah 5:2). 
 7. Nahum - The Prophet of Nineveh’s Doom ● A sequel to Jonah, foretelling Nineveh’s destruction. 
 89

8. Habakkuk - The Prophet Who Questioned God ● “The just shall live by faith” (Habakkuk 2:4), quoted in Romans, 
Galatians,
 
and
 
Hebrews.
 
 9. Zephaniah - The Prophet of the Day of the Lord ● Prophesied about God’s future judgment and restoration . 
 10. Haggai - The Prophet of Rebuilding ● Encouraged Israel to rebuild the temple after exile. 
 11. Zechariah - The Prophet of the Coming King ● Prophesied Jesus' triumphal entry (Zechariah 9:9). ● Contains more Messianic prophecies than any Minor Prophet. 
 12. Malachi - The Last Prophet ● Warned about spiritual apathy and foretold John the Baptist 
(Malachi
 
3:1).
 ● Closes the Old Testament, setting the stage for Christ. 
 Devotional Reflection The prophets were not just messengers of judgment; they were voices of 
hope,
 
pointing
 
to
 
Jesus
 
Christ.
 
Their
 
prophecies
 
show
 
God’s
 
justice,
 
mercy,
 
and
 
faithfulness
.
 
In
 
every
 
warning
 
of
 
judgment,
 
there
 
was
 
a
 
promise
 
of
 
restoration
 
through
 
the
 
coming
 
Messiah.
 ● Jesus is the fulfillment of prophecy. Every Major and Minor 
Prophet,
 
in
 
some
 
way,
 
pointed
 
to
 
Christ.
 ● God speaks through His Word today. Just as He used prophets, 
He
 
still
 
speaks
 
through
 
Scripture.
 ● Prophecy builds our faith. The accuracy of biblical prophecy 
affirms
 
the
 
trustworthiness
 
of
 
God’s
 
Word.
 
 Assignment 1. Read Isaiah 53 and write a reflection on how it points to Jesus. 90

2. Study Joel 2:28-32 and Acts 2:16-21. How does Peter confirm the 
fulfillment
 
of
 
Joel’s
 
prophecy
 
at
 
Pentecost?
 Conclusion The prophetic books reveal God’s heart for His people , His call to 
holiness,
 
His
 
plan
 
of
 
redemption,
 
and
 
His
 
promise
 
of
 
restoration.
 
As
 
believers,
 
studying
 
the
 
Major
 
and
 
Minor
 
Prophets
 
strengthens
 
our
 
understanding
 
of
 
God’s
 
character
 
and
 
deepens
 
our
 
faith
 
in
 
Jesus,
 
the
 
ultimate
 
fulfillment
 
of
 
prophecy.
 
91

Believers' Class: Week 22 TOPIC: The Gospels & Acts - The Life of Jesus and the Early Church 
(Matthew
 
to
 
John,
 
Acts)
 Introduction The Gospels and the Book of Acts are foundational to our understanding of 
Jesus
 
Christ
 
and
 
the
 
birth
 
of
 
His
 
Church.
 
The
 
Gospels
 
(Matthew,
 
Mark,
 
Luke,
 
and
 
John)
 
present
 
four
 
accounts
 
of
 
the
 
life,
 
ministry,
 
death,
 
and
 
resurrection
 
of
 
Jesus.
 
The
 
Book
 
of
 
Acts
 
continues
 
the
 
narrative
 
by
 
detailing
 
the
 
birth
 
and
 
expansion
 
of
 
the
 
early
 
Church
 
through
 
the
 
work
 
of
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit.
 Studying these books gives us a clearer picture of Jesus’ mission, His 
teachings,
 
His
 
sacrifice,
 
and
 
the
 
power
 
of
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit
 
working
 
through
 
His
 
followers.
 
Our
 
faith
 
is
 
rooted
 
in
 
Christ,
 
and
 
these
 
books
 
help
 
us
 
to
 
grow
 
in
 
our
 
knowledge
 
and
 
relationship
 
with
 
Him.
 The Four Gospels: Four Perspectives, One Story Each Gospel presents a unique perspective of Jesus’ life, yet they all 
harmonize
 
to
 
give
 
us
 
a
 
complete
 
picture
 
of
 
His
 
mission.
 1. The Gospel of Matthew - Jesus as the Promised King ● Written by Matthew (Levi), a tax collector turned disciple. ● Emphasizes Jesus as the Messianic King and the fulfillment of Old 
Testament
 
prophecies.
 ● Contains the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5-7), which teaches 
Kingdom
 
principles.
 ● Written primarily to a Jewish audience to prove that Jesus is the 
long-awaited
 
Messiah.
 
 2. The Gospel of Mark - Jesus as the Servant ● The shortest Gospel, likely written by John Mark, who worked 
closely
 
with
 
Peter.
 
92

● Emphasizes Jesus as a servant who came to give His life as a 
ransom
 
for
 
many
 
(Mark
 
10:45).
 ● Focuses on action and miracles , making it a fast-paced narrative. ● Written primarily for a Roman audience unfamiliar with Jewish 
traditions.
 
 3. The Gospel of Luke - Jesus as the Son of Man ● Written by Luke, a physician and companion of Paul. ● Presents Jesus as the perfect Son of Man , emphasizing His 
compassion
 
and
 
humanity.
 ● Contains the most detailed account of Jesus’ birth (Luke 1-2). ● Highlights Jesus’ ministry to the poor, outcasts, and Gentiles. ● Written for a Greek audience and emphasizes historical accuracy. 
 4. The Gospel of John - Jesus as the Son of God ● Written by John, the beloved disciple. ● Emphasizes Jesus’ divinity and eternal existence (John 1:1-14). ● Contains seven “I AM” statements that reveal Jesus’ divine nature 
(e.g.,
 
“I
 
am
 
the
 
Bread
 
of
 
Life”
 
-
 
John
 
6:35).
 ● Focuses on belief and salvation, with John 3:16 being a central 
verse.
 
 The Gospels give us a complete picture of Jesus, King, Servant, Son of 
Man,
 
and
 
Son
 
of
 
God,
 
so
 
that
 
we
 
may
 
believe
 
and
 
have
 
eternal
 
life
 
(John
 
20:31).
 The Book of Acts - The Birth of the Church The Book of Acts, written by Luke, serves as a bridge between the Gospels 
and
 
the
 
Epistles.
 
It
 
details
 
the
 
work
 
of
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit
 
in
 
establishing
 
the
 
Church
 
and
 
spreading
 
the
 
Gospel
 
from
 
Jerusalem
 
to
 
the
 
ends
 
of
 
the
 
earth
 
(Acts
 
1:8).
 Key Themes in Acts: 93

1. The Great Commission Fulfilled - The disciples carry out Jesus’ 
command
 
to
 
make
 
disciples
 
of
 
all
 
nations
 
(Matthew
 
28:19-20).
 2. The Power of the Holy Spirit - Acts 2 records the Pentecost , 
when
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit
 
descended
 
upon
 
the
 
believers,
 
empowering
 
them
 
for
 
ministry.
 3. The Spread of the Gospel - The apostles, especially Peter and 
Paul,
 
take
 
the
 
message
 
of
 
Christ
 
beyond
 
Jewish
 
boundaries
 
to
 
the
 
Gentile
 
world.
 4. Persecution and Growth - Despite opposition, the Church grows 
rapidly,
 
proving
 
that
 
the
 
Gospel
 
cannot
 
be
 
stopped.
 5. Paul’s Missionary Journeys - Acts follows Paul’s journeys as he 
plants
 
churches
 
and
 
writes
 
letters
 
that
 
later
 
become
 
much
 
of
 
the
 
New
 
Testament.
 Acts shows us that the Gospel is not just history, it is a living, powerful 
movement
 
that
 
continues
 
through
 
us
 
today
.
 Devotional Reflection ● Have you encountered Jesus in a personal way through the 
Gospels?
 ● Are you living in the power of the Holy Spirit like the early 
Church?
 ● Are you actively sharing the Gospel and making disciples as 
Jesus
 
commanded?
 Reading the Gospels and Acts with an open heart will transform your faith 
and
 
make
 
you
 
an
 
effective
 
witness
 
for
 
Christ.
 Assignment 1. Read one Gospel over the next week (choose Matthew, Mark, Luke, 
or
 
John).
 2. Write a short reflection on how Jesus’ teachings and actions have 
impacted
 
your
 
faith.
 
 
94

By immersing ourselves in the life of Jesus and the early Church, we are 
equipped
 
to
 
live
 
as
 
bold
 
witnesses
 
for
 
Christ
 
in
 
our
 
own
 
time.
 Conclusion The Gospels and Acts lay the foundation for our faith. Jesus came to 
redeem
 
us,
 
and
 
through
 
the
 
power
 
of
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit,
 
we
 
are
 
now
 
His
 
hands
 
and
 
feet
 
in
 
the
 
world.
 
Let
 
us
 
take
 
His
 
message
 
to
 
the
 
nations,
 
just
 
as
 
the
 
early
 
Church
 
did,
 
knowing
 
that
 
the
 
same
 
Spirit
 
who
 
empowered
 
them
 
empowers
 
us
 
today.
 Let’s go forth, living in the reality of Jesus Christ and the power of the 
Holy
 
Spirit,
 
continuing
 
the
 
story
 
of
 
Acts
 
in
 
our
 
generation.
 
Amen!
95

Believers' Class: Week 23 TOPIC: The Epistles (Pauline & General) - Apostolic Teachings 
(Romans
 
to
 
Jude)
 Introduction The Epistles are letters written by the apostles to early churches and 
believers,
 
providing
 
doctrinal
 
instruction,
 
encouragement,
 
correction,
 
and
 
practical
 
guidance
 
for
 
Christian
 
living.
 
They
 
form
 
the
 
theological
 
foundation
 
of
 
the
 
New
 
Testament,
 
explaining
 
the
 
significance
 
of
 
Jesus’
 
life,
 
death,
 
and
 
resurrection,
 
and
 
instructing
 
believers
 
on
 
how
 
to
 
live
 
in
 
light
 
of
 
the
 
Gospel.
 The Epistles can be divided into two main categories: 1. Pauline Epistles (Romans to Philemon) - Letters written by 
Apostle
 
Paul.
 2. General Epistles (Hebrews to Jude) - Letters written by other 
apostles
 
and
 
early
 
church
 
leaders.
 
 Understanding these writings is crucial to growing in faith, living a godly 
life,
 
and
 
being
 
equipped
 
to
 
disciple
 
others.
 1. The Pauline Epistles (Romans - Philemon) Paul wrote 13 (or 14, if including Hebrews) epistles to churches and 
individuals,
 
addressing
 
theology,
 
Christian
 
conduct,
 
and
 
church
 
leadership.
 
His
 
letters
 
can
 
be
 
divided
 
into
 
three
 
categories:
 A. Doctrinal Epistles (Romans, 1 & 2 Corinthians, Galatians) ● Romans: A comprehensive explanation of salvation by grace 
through
 
faith,
 
justification,
 
and
 
the
 
righteousness
 
of
 
God.
 ● 1 Corinthians: Addresses divisions in the church, spiritual gifts, 
love,
 
and
 
resurrection.
 ● 2 Corinthians: Focuses on Paul’s ministry, suffering, and 
encouragement
 
for
 
believers.
 
96

● Galatians: Defends salvation by grace through faith, opposing 
legalism
 
and
 
emphasizing
 
freedom
 
in
 
Christ.
 
 B. Prison Epistles (Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, Philemon) ● Ephesians: Teaches about the believer’s identity in Christ, spiritual 
blessings,
 
and
 
unity
 
in
 
the
 
body
 
of
 
Christ.
 ● Philippians: A letter of joy, gratitude, and encouragement to 
remain
 
faithful
 
despite
 
trials.
 ● Colossians: Exalts Christ as supreme and warns against false 
teachings.
 ● Philemon: A personal letter advocating forgiveness and 
reconciliation.
 
 C. Pastoral Epistles (1 & 2 Timothy, Titus) ● 1 & 2 Timothy, Titus: Instructions for church leadership, sound 
doctrine,
 
and
 
godly
 
living.
 
 2. The General Epistles (Hebrews - Jude) These letters were written to a broader audience, addressing faith, 
endurance,
 
and
 
warnings
 
against
 
false
 
teachings.
 ● Hebrews: Highlights Jesus as the fulfillment of the Old Testament, 
our
 
High
 
Priest,
 
and
 
the
 
New
 
Covenant.
 ● James: Stresses practical Christian living, faith demonstrated by 
works,
 
and
 
perseverance.
 ● 1 & 2 Peter: Encourages suffering believers, warns against false 
teachers,
 
and
 
speaks
 
of
 
Christ’s
 
return.
 ● 1, 2, & 3 John: Focuses on love, truth, and assurance of salvation. ● Jude: Warns against apostasy and contending for the faith. 
 Devotional Reflection 1. Are you living according to the teachings of the Epistles? The 
letters
 
of
 
Paul
 
and
 
other
 
apostles
 
call
 
us
 
to
 
holiness,
 
faith,
 
and
 
love.
 
97

2. Do you understand your identity in Christ? The Epistles reveal 
our
 
position
 
as
 
saved,
 
sanctified,
 
and
 
seated
 
in
 
heavenly
 
places
 
with
 
Christ
 
(Ephesians
 
2:6).
 3. Are you growing in sound doctrine? False teachings existed in 
the
 
early
 
church
 
and
 
still
 
exist
 
today.
 
Staying
 
rooted
 
in
 
Scripture
 
protects
 
us.
 
 Assignment 1. Choose one Epistle and read it this week. 2. Write down three key takeaways from your reading and share them 
with
 
someone.
 
 Conclusion The Epistles provide guidance for every believer. They teach us how to 
live,
 
endure
 
trials,
 
avoid
 
deception,
 
and
 
fulfill
 
the
 
Great
 
Commission.
 
Let
 
us
 
apply
 
these
 
truths
 
in
 
our
 
lives,
 
standing
 
firm
 
in
 
sound
 
doctrine
 
and
 
spreading
 
the
 
message
 
of
 
Christ
 
to
 
the
 
world.
 
Amen!
 
98

Daniel,

(Understanding
 
Prophecy
 
and
 
End
 
Times)

His
 
people,
 
His
 
judgment
 
against
 
sin,
 
and
 
His
 
promises
 
of
 
restoration.
 
Apocalyptic
 
literature,

subset
 
of

revelations

future
 
events
 
through
 
vivid
 
imagery,

and

The
 
books
 
of
 
Daniel,

some
 
of

most

messages

,
 
particularly
 
those

Israel
 
and
 
the

 2. The Book of Ezekiel - God’s Judgment and Restoration Key Passages: Ezekiel 1, 10, 37, 40-48 Ezekiel was a prophet during the Babylonian exile, and his visions

the

 3. The Book of Revelation - The Victory of Christ Key Passages: Revelation 1, 5, 13, 19-22 Revelation is not just about the future but is a letter of encouragement 
to
 
the
 
persecuted
 
Church.

King

and

plan
 
for
 
redemption
.

Believers' Class: Week 25 TOPIC: How to Rightly Divide the Word - Context, Exegesis vs. Eisegesis (2 Timothy 2:15) Introduction In 2 Timothy 2:15, the Apostle Paul urges believers to "rightly divide the 
word
 
of
 
truth."
 
This
 
is
 
a
 
call
 
to
 
approach
 
Scripture
 
with
 
diligence,
 
care,
 
and
 
reverence,
 
ensuring
 
that
 
we
 
understand
 
and
 
interpret
 
it
 
accurately.
 
The
 
study
 
of
 
God’s
 
Word
 
is
 
not
 
a
 
casual
 
or
 
haphazard
 
endeavor;
 
it
 
requires
 
intentionality,
 
humility,
 
and
 
a
 
commitment
 
to
 
sound
 
methods
 
of
 
interpretation.
 In this lesson, we will explore the importance of rightly dividing the 
Word
 
of
 
truth
,
 
focusing
 
on
 
the
 
key
 
aspects
 
of
 
context
,
 
exegesis
,
 
and
 
eisegesis
.
 
Our
 
aim
 
is
 
to
 
equip
 
you
 
with
 
tools
 
for
 
responsible
 
biblical
 
interpretation,
 
so
 
that
 
you
 
may
 
handle
 
Scripture
 
with
 
integrity
 
and
 
apply
 
it
 
correctly
 
in
 
your
 
life.
 1. The Importance of Context One of the first and most essential principles in interpreting the Bible is the 
importance
 
of
 
context
.
 
Context
 
is
 
key
 
to
 
understanding
 
the
 
meaning
 
of
 
any
 
passage
 
of
 
Scripture.
 
Without
 
proper
 
context,
 
we
 
can
 
easily
 
misinterpret
 
what
 
God
 
is
 
saying,
 
leading
 
to
 
confusion
 
or
 
misapplication.
 A. Immediate Context The immediate context refers to the verses immediately surrounding the 
passage
 
you
 
are
 
studying.
 
A
 
verse
 
should
 
never
 
be
 
taken
 
in
 
isolation;
 
it
 
must
 
be
 
understood
 
in
 
light
 
of
 
the
 
verses
 
before
 
and
 
after
 
it.
 
Always
 
ask
 
the
 
questions:
 
What
 
is
 
the
 
immediate
 
context?
 
What
 
is
 
the
 
flow
 
of
 
thought
 
in
 
the
 
passage?
 For example, consider Philippians 4:13: "I can do all things through Christ 
who
 
strengthens
 
me."
 
While
 
this
 
verse
 
is
 
often
 
quoted
 
to
 
encourage
 
personal
 
achievement,
 
we
 
must
 
remember
 
that
 
Paul
 
was
 
speaking
 
in
 
the
 
104

context of contentment in all circumstances, not necessarily success in 
every
 
endeavor.
 
The
 
surrounding
 
verses
 
show
 
that
 
Paul
 
is
 
talking
 
about
 
endurance
 
through
 
hardship
 
and
 
the
 
strength
 
Christ
 
provides
 
to
 
endure.
 B. Literary Context In addition to the immediate context, we also need to consider the literary 
context
.
 
Is
 
the
 
passage
 
a
 
historical
 
narrative,
 
a
 
poetic
 
song,
 
a
 
prophetic
 
utterance,
 
or
 
a
 
letter
 
of
 
instruction?
 
Different
 
literary
 
genres
 
in
 
the
 
Bible
 
require
 
different
 
interpretative
 
approaches.
 
Understanding
 
whether
 
you
 
are
 
reading
 
a
 
parable,
 
a
 
prophecy,
 
or
 
a
 
letter
 
will
 
shape
 
your
 
understanding.
 For example, when reading the Psalms, we must recognize that they are 
poetic
 
and
 
often
 
contain
 
figures
 
of
 
speech.
 
In
 
contrast,
 
when
 
reading
 
historical
 
narratives
 
like
 
the
 
Book
 
of
 
Acts,
 
the
 
focus
 
is
 
on
 
literal
 
events
 
and
 
real
 
people.
 C. Historical and Cultural Context We must also take into account the historical and cultural context of the 
time
 
when
 
the
 
text
 
was
 
written.
 
The
 
Bible
 
was
 
written
 
in
 
specific
 
historical
 
and
 
cultural
 
settings,
 
and
 
understanding
 
those
 
can
 
illuminate
 
the
 
meaning
 
of
 
certain
 
passages.
 
For
 
instance,
 
knowing
 
the
 
customs
 
of
 
ancient
 
Israel
 
helps
 
us
 
understand
 
rituals
 
in
 
Levitical
 
law,
 
or
 
understanding
 
the
 
Greco-Roman
 
world
 
helps
 
explain
 
many
 
of
 
Paul’s
 
writings
 
in
 
the
 
New
 
Testament.
 2. Exegesis vs. Eisegesis To rightly divide the Word of truth, we must understand the difference 
between
 
exegesis
 
and
 
eisegesis
,
 
two
 
approaches
 
to
 
interpreting
 
Scripture.
 A. Exegesis - Drawing Out the Meaning Exegesis is the correct method of interpretation. It comes from the Greek 
word
 
exēgeisthai
,
 
which
 
means
 
“to
 
lead
 
out”
 
or
 
“to
 
explain.”
 
In
 
exegesis,
 
the
 
interpreter
 
seeks
 
to
 
draw
 
out
 
the
 
meaning
 
of
 
the
 
text
 
as
 
it
 
was
 
105

originally intended by the author, guided by the Holy Spirit. It involves 
careful
 
study,
 
including
 
examining
 
the
 
context,
 
language,
 
grammar,
 
and
 
culture
 
of
 
the
 
time.
 Key elements of exegesis include: ● Understanding the author’s intent : What did the author intend to 
communicate
 
to
 
the
 
original
 
audience?
 
What
 
are
 
the
 
key
 
themes
 
and
 
messages
 
of
 
the
 
passage?
 
 ● Looking at the original languages : Understanding the Hebrew, 
Greek,
 
or
 
Aramaic
 
words
 
can
 
help
 
clarify
 
nuances
 
in
 
meaning.
 
 ● Consulting historical and cultural context : Understanding the 
time
 
and
 
culture
 
in
 
which
 
the
 
text
 
was
 
written
 
is
 
crucial
 
for
 
proper
 
interpretation.
 
 Exegesis enables us to hear the Word of God as it was intended to be 
heard,
 
with
 
our
 
focus
 
on
 
the
 
meaning
 
that
 
God
 
has
 
given.
 B. Eisegesis - Reading Into the Text On the other hand, eisegesis is the wrong approach to interpreting the 
Bible.
 
It
 
comes
 
from
 
the
 
Greek
 
word
 
eis
 
meaning
 
“into,”
 
and
 
it
 
refers
 
to
 
reading
 
our
 
own
 
ideas
 
and
 
biases
 
into
 
the
 
text
 
rather
 
than
 
drawing
 
the
 
meaning
 
out
 
of
 
it.
 When we practice eisegesis , we approach Scripture with preconceived 
notions
 
or
 
agendas
 
and
 
force
 
the
 
text
 
to
 
fit
 
our
 
own
 
desires
 
or
 
perspectives.
 
This
 
can
 
lead
 
to
 
misinterpretations
 
that
 
distort
 
the
 
truth
 
of
 
God’s
 
Word.
 For example, if we read our cultural preferences or personal desires into a 
passage,
 
we
 
may
 
end
 
up
 
interpreting
 
it
 
in
 
a
 
way
 
that
 
suits
 
our
 
own
 
preferences
 
rather
 
than
 
allowing
 
God’s
 
message
 
to
 
speak
 
directly
 
to
 
us.
 Eisegesis is dangerous because it distorts the true meaning of Scripture and 
leads
 
to
 
false
 
teachings.
 
106

3. The Importance of Rightly Dividing the Word To rightly divide the Word of truth means to handle the Scriptures 
accurately
 
and
 
faithfully.
 
We
 
are
 
responsible
 
before
 
God
 
to
 
interpret
 
His
 
Word
 
correctly
 
and
 
to
 
apply
 
it
 
in
 
our
 
lives.
 
When
 
we
 
divide
 
the
 
Word
 
properly:
 ● We avoid false doctrine and misleading teachings. ● We gain a clearer understanding of God’s will for our lives. ● We are better equipped to live out the teachings of Scripture. ● We fulfill our calling to be faithful stewards of the Word of God. 
 As believers, it is essential that we engage in careful study of Scripture, 
striving
 
to
 
rightly
 
divide
 
the
 
Word
 
through
 
prayer,
 
humility,
 
and
 
reliance
 
on
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit.
 Devotional Reflection As you read and study the Bible, remember that it is not just a book, it is 
the
 
living
 
Word
 
of
 
God
.
 
Approach
 
it
 
with
 
reverence,
 
asking
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit
 
to
 
guide
 
you
 
into
 
all
 
truth.
 
When
 
you
 
come
 
across
 
difficult
 
passages,
 
take
 
time
 
to
 
understand
 
the
 
context,
 
and
 
always
 
seek
 
to
 
draw
 
out
 
the
 
meaning
 
rather
 
than
 
impose
 
your
 
own
 
interpretation.
 Let us pray that God will give us wisdom to handle His Word faithfully, 
and
 
that
 
we
 
will
 
grow
 
in
 
our
 
understanding
 
of
 
Him
 
as
 
we
 
study
 
His
 
Word.
 Assignment 1. Read 2 Timothy 2:15 and reflect on what it means to "rightly 
divide
 
the
 
Word
 
of
 
truth."
 2. Choose a passage you have found challenging and apply the 
principles
 
of
 
context
,
 
exegesis
,
 
and
 
eisegesis
.
 
Write
 
a
 
brief
 
reflection
 
on
 
what
 
you
 
learned
 
from
 
the
 
passage
 
after

these
 
principles.
 
 By the end of this study, may we all grow in our ability to handle God's 
Word
 
rightly
 
and
 
live
 
out
 
His
 
truth
 
in
 
our
 
daily
 
lives.
 
107

Conclusion In conclusion, rightly dividing the Word of God requires diligence, 
humility,
 
and
 
a
 
commitment
 
to
 
accuracy.
 
As
 
we
 
seek
 
to
 
understand
 
the
 
Scriptures,
 
let
 
us
 
avoid
 
the
 
temptation
 
of
 
reading
 
into

text
 
what

not

Instead,
 
let

to

guidance

Holy
 
Spirit.
 
When
 
we

divide
 
the

we
 
honor
 
God
 
and
 
grow
 
in
 
our
 
faith.

Believers' Class: Week 27 TOPIC: The Covenants of God - Old vs. New Covenant (Jeremiah 
31:31-34,
 
Hebrews
 
8:6-13)
 Introduction A covenant is a divine agreement between God and His people, 
establishing
 
the
 
terms
 
of
 
their
 
relationship.
 
The
 
Bible
 
speaks
 
of
 
multiple
 
covenants,
 
but
 
the
 
Old
 
and
 
New
 
Covenants
 
stand
 
as
 
the
 
foundation
 
of
 
God's
 
redemptive
 
plan.
 
Understanding
 
these
 
covenants
 
is
 
key
 
to
 
rightly
 
interpreting
 
Scripture
 
and
 
applying
 
it
 
to
 
our
 
lives.
 1. The Old Covenant - The Law Given to Israel Key Passages: Exodus 19-24, Deuteronomy 28, Jeremiah 31:31-32 The Old Covenant was established at Mount Sinai when God gave the Law 
to
 
Israel
 
through
 
Moses.
 
It
 
was
 
a
 
covenant
 
of
 
obedience:
 
blessings
 
followed
 
obedience,
 
and
 
curses
 
followed
 
disobedience
 
(Deuteronomy
 
28).
 Characteristics of the Old Covenant: ● Based on the Law (613 commandments, including the Ten 
Commandments).
 ● Required sacrifices for atonement (Leviticus 16). ● Had a mediator, Moses (Exodus 24:8). ● Written on stone tablets (Exodus 31:18). ● Conditional - dependent on Israel’s obedience (Deuteronomy 
28:1-2).
 
 The problem with the Old Covenant was not the Law itself but Israel’s 
inability
 
to
 
keep
 
it
 
(Hebrews
 
8:7-8).
 
It
 
pointed
 
to
 
the
 
need
 
for
 
something
 
greater,
 
a
 
covenant
 
written
 
on
 
the
 
heart.
 2. The New Covenant - A Better Covenant Key Passages: Jeremiah 31:31-34, Hebrews 8:6-13, Luke 22:20 
114

The New Covenant was prophesied in the Old Testament and fulfilled in 
Christ.
 
It
 
is
 
a
 
covenant
 
of
 
grace,
 
mediated
 
by
 
Jesus,
 
bringing
 
an
 
internal
 
transformation
 
rather
 
than
 
mere
 
external
 
adherence
 
to
 
laws.
 Characteristics of the New Covenant: ● Established through Jesus’ blood (Luke 22:20). ● The law is written on the heart, not on tablets of stone (Jeremiah 
31:33).
 ● Provides full forgiveness of sins (Hebrews 8:12, 10:17-18). ● Has a perfect mediator, Jesus (Hebrews 8:6). ● Unconditional - based on God's promise, not human effort 
(Ephesians
 
2:8-9).
 
 The New Covenant fulfills the Old, not by abolishing the moral principles 
of
 
God’s
 
law,
 
but
 
by
 
bringing
 
about
 
true
 
righteousness
 
through
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit
 
(Romans
 
8:3-4).
 3.
 
Comparing
 
the
 
Old
 
and
 
New
 
Covenants
 
Aspect Old Covenant New Covenant 
Mediator Moses Jesus Christ (Hebrews 8:6) 
Law Written On Stone tablets Human hearts (Jeremiah 31:33) 
Sacrifices Repeated animal sacrifices Christ's once-for-all sacrifice (Hebrews 10:10-12) 
Access to God Through priests Direct access through Christ (Hebrews 4:16) 
Forgiveness Temporary, through animal sacrifices 
Permanent, through Christ’s blood (Hebrews 10:17-18) 
 115

4. Application - Living in the New Covenant As believers, we must walk in the reality of the New Covenant, 
understanding
 
that
 
our
 
righteousness
 
is
 
in
 
Christ,
 
not
 
in
 
works
 
of
 
the
 
law.
 
However,
 
this
 
does
 
not
 
mean
 
we
 
live
 
lawlessly,
 
but
 
rather,
 
we
 
obey
 
God
 
from
 
a
 
transformed
 
heart
 
(Titus
 
2:11-12).
 Devotional Reflection: ● Are you living in the freedom and grace of the New Covenant? ● Do you still relate to God based on performance, or do you rest in 
Christ’s
 
finished
 
work?
 
 Conclusion The Old Covenant served its purpose in revealing sin and pointing to 
Christ,
 
but
 
the
 
New
 
Covenant
 
brings
 
the
 
fullness
 
of
 
salvation
 
and
 
an
 
unbreakable

119:105,

1:22)

guide,

and

 Conclusion Living by the Word means more than just reading it, it means allowing it to

As

Believers' Class: Week 29 TOPIC: The Trinity - Understanding the Godhead (Matthew 28:19, 2 Corinthians 13:14) Introduction The doctrine of the Trinity is one of the most profound and foundational 
teachings
 
of
 
the
 
Christian
 
faith.
 
It
 
defines
 
the
 
nature
 
of
 
God
 
as
 
three
 
distinct
 
Persons,
 
Father,
 
Son,
 
and
 
Holy
 
Spirit,
 
yet
 
one
 
in
 
essence.
 
 Matthew 28:19 affirms this truth: "Go ye therefore, and teach all nations, baptizing them in the name of the 
Father,
 
and
 
of
 
the
 
Son,
 
and
 
of
 
the
 
Holy
 
Ghost."
 
 Likewise, 2 Corinthians 13:14 expresses the relational dynamic within the 
Godhead:
 
 "The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the 
communion
 
of
 
the
 
Holy
 
Ghost,
 
be
 
with
 
you
 
all."
 Understanding the Trinity is essential for our faith because it shapes how 
we
 
relate
 
to
 
God,
 
worship
 
Him,
 
and
 
understand
 
His
 
work
 
in
 
salvation.
 I. The Biblical Basis for the Trinity Though the word "Trinity" is not found in Scripture, the concept is deeply 
embedded
 
in
 
biblical
 
revelation.
 1. The Father is God - Jesus referred to the Father as God (John 
6:27,
 
1
 
Peter
 
1:2).
 2. The Son is God - Jesus is called God (John 1:1, Hebrews 1:8). 3. The Holy Spirit is God - He is identified as God and possesses 
divine
 
attributes
 
(Acts
 
5:3-4,
 
1
 
Corinthians
 
2:10-11).
 
121

4. The Three are Distinct Yet One - At Jesus’ baptism (Matthew 
3:16-17),
 
all
 
three
 
Persons
 
of
 
the
 
Trinity
 
are
 
present
 
and
 
active.
 
 II. Understanding the Oneness and Distinction in the Godhead A. One in Essence, Three in Person ● The Trinity is not three gods (polytheism) nor one God acting in 
different
 
modes
 
(modalism).
 
The
 
Father,
 
Son,
 
and
 
Spirit
 
are
 
distinct
 
yet
 
share
 
the
 
same
 
divine
 
nature.
 ● Analogy: Water exists as ice, liquid, and vapor but remains H ₂ O. 
Though
 
imperfect,
 
this
 
analogy
 
helps
 
illustrate
 
the
 
unity
 
and
 
distinction
 
within
 
the
 
Godhead.
 
 B. The Roles of Each Person in Redemption ● The Father planned salvation and sent the Son (John 3:16, 
Ephesians
 
1:3-6).
 ● The Son accomplished salvation through His life, death, and 
resurrection
 
(John
 
10:11,
 
1
 
John
 
2:2).
 ● The Holy Spirit applies salvation by regenerating, indwelling, and 
sanctifying
 
believers
 
(John
 
14:26,
 
Titus
 
3:5).
 
 III. The Trinity in Christian Living 1. Prayer and Worship - We pray to the Father, through the Son, by 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit
 
(Ephesians
 
2:18).
 2. Christian Fellowship - The unity in the Godhead models our unity 
in
 
the
 
body
 
of
 
Christ
 
(John
 
17:21).
 3. Empowerment for Ministry - The Spirit empowers us to live as 
witnesses
 
of
 
Christ
 
(Acts
 
1:8).
 4. Security in Salvation - The Father secures, the Son intercedes, and 
the
 
Spirit
 
seals
 
us
 
(Romans
 
8:33-39,
 
Ephesians
 
1:13-14).
 
 IV. Devotional Reflection 
122

The Trinity reveals God’s deep love and commitment to His people. Each 
Person
 
of
 
the
 
Godhead
 
is
 
actively
 
involved
 
in
 
our
 
salvation
 
and
 
sanctification.
 
Take
 
a
 
moment
 
to
 
reflect:
 ● Do you recognize the Father’s love for you? ● Have you placed full trust in the finished work of Christ? ● Are you walking in the power of the Holy Spirit? 
 The Trinity is not just a theological concept; it is the reality of our 
relationship
 
with
 
God.
 Assignment 1. Read John 14:16-26 and identify the roles of each Person of the 
Trinity
 
in
 
our
 
lives.
 2. Memorize 2 Corinthians 13:14 and reflect on how you have 
experienced
 
the
 
grace,
 
love,
 
and
 
fellowship
 
of
 
the
 
Trinity.
 
 Conclusion The doctrine of the Trinity is at the heart of the Christian faith. As we grow 
in
 
our
 
understanding
 
of
 
God’s
 
triune
 
nature,
 
we
 
grow
 
in
 
intimacy
 
with
 
Him.
 
Let
 
us
 
worship
 
and
 
serve
 
God
 
with
 
the
 
knowledge
 
that
 
He
 
is
 
Father,
 
Son,
 
and
 
Holy
 
Spirit,
 
three
 
in
 
one,
 
and
 
one
 
in
 
three.
 
Amen.
 
123

Believers' Class: Week 30 The Nature of Jesus Christ - Fully God, Fully Man 
 
(John
 
1:1-14,
 
Colossians
 
2:9)
 Introduction One of the most profound truths of the Christian faith is the dual nature of 
Jesus
 
Christ,
 
He
 
is
 
fully
 
God
 
and
 
fully
 
man.
 
This
 
is
 
not
 
a
 
contradiction
 
but
 
a
 
divine
 
mystery
 
that
 
holds
 
essential
 
significance
 
for
 
our
 
salvation,
 
relationship
 
with
 
God,
 
and
 
understanding
 
of
 
Christ’s
 
present
 
work
 
in
 
heaven.
 John 1:1 declares, "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with 
God,
 
and
 
the
 
Word
 
was
 
God."
 
Then
 
verse
 
14
 
confirms,
 
"And
 
the
 
Word
 
was
 
made
 
flesh,
 
and
 
dwelt
 
among
 
us…"
 
Likewise,
 
Colossians
 
2:9
 
states,
 
"For
 
in
 
him
 
dwelleth
 
all
 
the
 
fulness
 
of
 
the
 
Godhead
 
bodily."
 
These
 
passages
 
affirm
 
both
 
His
 
divine
 
and
 
human
 
natures.
 Jesus Christ as Fully Man During His Earthly Ministry 1. His Birth in the Flesh ○ Jesus was born of a virgin, fulfilling Isaiah’s prophecy 
(Isaiah
 
7:14;
 
Matthew
 
1:23).
 ○ He experienced human limitations, hunger (Matthew 4:2), 
weariness
 
(John
 
4:6),
 
and
 
sorrow
 
(John
 
11:35).
 
 2. His Human Emotions and Temptations ○ Jesus felt deep emotions: compassion (Matthew 9:36), joy 
(Luke
 
10:21),
 
and
 
distress
 
(Luke
 
22:44).
 ○ He was tempted in all ways yet without sin (Hebrews 4:15). 
 3. His Dependence on the Father and the Spirit ○ Jesus operated within human limitations, relying on the 
Holy
 
Spirit
 
(Luke
 
4:1,
 
18).
 
124

○ He prayed constantly, modeling dependence on God (Mark 
1:35;
 
Luke
 
5:16).
 
 4. His Death as a True Human Sacrifice ○ His humanity enabled Him to suffer and die as our atoning 
sacrifice
 
(Philippians
 
2:8;
 
Hebrews
 
2:14-17).
 ○ His physical death on the cross was necessary for our 
redemption
 
(1
 
Peter
 
3:18).
 Jesus Christ as Fully God During His Earthly Ministry 1. His Divine Authority ○ Jesus forgave sins, a prerogative of God alone (Mark 2:5-7). ○ He commanded nature, calming the storm (Mark 4:39) and 
walking
 
on
 
water
 
(Matthew
 
14:25).
 
 2. His Power Over Death and Demons ○ He raised the dead, demonstrating divine power (John 
11:43-44).
 ○ Demons recognized His divine authority (Mark 1:24). 
 3. His Self-Identification as God ○ He declared, "Before Abraham was, I am" (John 8:58), 
claiming
 
eternal
 
existence.
 ○ Thomas worshiped Him as "My Lord and my God" (John 
20:28),
 
and
 
Jesus
 
accepted
 
it.
 
 The Exalted Christ - His Present Role in Heaven 1. His Exaltation and Lordship ○ After His resurrection, Jesus ascended to heaven and was 
seated
 
at
 
the
 
right
 
hand
 
of
 
the
 
Father
 
(Acts
 
1:9-11;
 
Ephesians
 
1:20-21).
 
125

○ Philippians 2:9-11 declares that He has been given a name 
above
 
all
 
names,
 
and
 
every
 
knee
 
shall
 
bow
 
before
 
Him.
 
 2. His Role as High Priest ○ Jesus intercedes for believers before the Father (Hebrews 
7:25;
 
Romans
 
8:34).
 ○ As our High Priest, He provides mercy and grace in times of 
need
 
(Hebrews
 
4:14-16).
 
 3. His Ongoing Work in the Church ○ Christ, as the head of the Church, directs and empowers His 
body
 
(Colossians
 
1:18).
 
 4. His Eternal Kingship ○ Jesus reigns now and will consummate His rule upon His 
return
 
(Revelation
 
19:11-16).
 ○ His kingdom is everlasting (Daniel 7:14; 1 Corinthians 
15:24-28).
 
 Devotional Reflection The dual nature of Christ is not just theology, it is the foundation of our 
faith.
 
As
 
fully
 
man,
 
He
 
understands
 
our
 
struggles,
 
and
 
as
 
fully
 
God,
 
He
 
has
 
the
 
power
 
to
 
save,
 
sustain,
 
and
 
lead
 
us.
 
Because
 
He
 
lived
 
as
 
we
 
live,
 
He
 
is
 
a
 
compassionate
 
High
 
Priest.
 
Because
 
He
 
reigns
 
as
 
God,
 
He
 
is
 
worthy
 
of
 
our
 
worship
 
and
 
obedience.
 Ask yourself: Do I relate to Jesus as my understanding Savior? Do I honor 
Him
 
as
 
my
 
reigning
 
King?
 Assignment 1. Read Philippians 2:5-11 and write down three ways Christ’s 
humility
 
and
 
exaltation
 
impact
 
your
 
faith.
 
126

2. Spend time in prayer, thanking Jesus for both His humanity and 
deity.
 
 Conclusion Jesus Christ is fully God and fully man, this is the mystery and beauty of 
our
 
faith.
 
He
 
walked
 
among
 
us,
 
suffered
 
for
 
us,
 
and
 
now
 
reigns
 
for
 
us.
 
As
 
we
 
grow
 
in
 
our
 
understanding
 
of
 
His
 
nature,
 
let
 
us
 
worship
 
Him
 
with
 
awe
 
and
 
follow
 
Him
 
with
 
unwavering
 
devotion.
 
May
 
our
 
lives
 
reflect
 
the
 
truth
 
of
 
who
 
He
 
is!
 “Jesus Christ the same yesterday, and to day, and for ever.” - Hebrews 
13:8
 
127

Believers' Class: Week 32 TOPIC: The Church - Universal & Local - The Body of Christ 
 
(1
 
Corinthians
 
12:12-27)
 Introduction The Church is not a building, denomination, or institution; it is the body of 
Christ.
 
The
 
Bible
 
presents
 
the
 
Church
 
in
 
two
 
main
 
ways:
 
the
 
Universal
 
Church,
 
which
 
includes
 
all
 
believers
 
across
 
time
 
and
 
space,
 
and
 
the
 
Local
 
Church,
 
which
 
is
 
a
 
specific
 
gathering
 
of
 
believers
 
in
 
a
 
particular
 
place.
 
Understanding
 
the
 
nature
 
and
 
function
 
of
 
the
 
Church
 
helps
 
believers
 
to
 
walk
 
in
 
unity,
 
purpose,
 
and
 
love.
 The Universal Church: The Body of Christ 1. What is the Universal Church? ○ The Universal Church consists of all people who have been 
born
 
again
 
by
 
faith
 
in
 
Jesus
 
Christ
 
(Ephesians
 
2:19-22).
 ○ It transcends culture, nationality, and denominational lines 
(Galatians
 
3:28).
 ○ It is described as the “Bride of Christ” (Revelation 19:7-9). 
 2. Christ as the Head of the Church ○ Christ is the foundation and head of the Church (Colossians 
1:18).
 ○ He purchased the Church with His blood (Acts 20:28). ○ The Church exists to glorify Christ and fulfill His mission 
(Matthew
 
28:19-20).
 
 3. The Church as One Body with Many Members ○ Paul describes the Church as a body with different parts 
functioning
 
together
 
(1
 
Corinthians
 
12:12-27).
 ○ Every believer has a role to play (Romans 12:4-5). 
131

○ Unity in diversity is the strength of the Church (Ephesians 
4:3-6).
 
 The Local Church: A Place of Fellowship and Growth 1. What is the Local Church? ○ A local church is a gathering of believers in a specific 
community
 
(Acts
 
2:42-47).
 ○ It provides discipleship, fellowship, worship, and ministry. ○ The local church functions under spiritual leadership 
(Hebrews
 
13:17).
 
 2. The Importance of Belonging to a Local Church ○ Encourages spiritual growth (Ephesians 4:11-16). ○ Provides accountability and pastoral care (Hebrews 
10:24-25).
 ○ Equips believers for service and ministry (Ephesians 4:12). 
 3. Responsibilities of Members in a Local Church ○ Commitment to sound doctrine (Acts 2:42). ○ Participation in corporate worship and prayer (Hebrews 
10:25).
 ○ Serving in love and humility (Galatians 5:13). ○ Financial stewardship and giving (2 Corinthians 9:7). 
 Devotional Reflection The Church is not a spectator event; it is a living, breathing body of 
believers
 
working
 
together
 
under
 
the
 
lordship
 
of
 
Jesus
 
Christ.
 
Are
 
you
 
actively
 
engaged
 
in
 
your
 
local
 
church?
 
Do
 
you
 
recognize
 
your
 
role
 
in
 
the
 
body
 
of
 
Christ?
 
The
 
Church
 
is
 
God's
 
chosen
 
vessel
 
to
 
reveal
 
His
 
wisdom
 
and
 
love
 
to
 
the
 
world
 
(Ephesians
 
3:10).
 
Commit
 
yourself
 
today
 
to
 
being
 
a
 
faithful
 
and
 
active
 
part
 
of
 
His
 
body.
 Assignment 132

1. Read 1 Corinthians 12 and reflect on what part of the body you 
identify
 
with.
 2. Write down three ways you can serve more effectively in your local 
church.
 3. Pray for the unity and growth of both the Universal and Local 
Church.
 
 Conclusion The Church is God's instrument for His purposes on earth. The Universal 
Church
 
represents
 
the
 
entire
 
body
 
of
 
believers,
 
while
 
the
 
Local
 
Church
 
provides
 
a
 
community
 
for
 
worship,
 
teaching,
 
and
 
spiritual
 
growth.
 
As
 
members
 
of
 
Christ’s
 
body,
 
we
 
are
 
called
 
to
 
function
 
in
 
love,
 
unity,
 
and
 
service.
 
May
 
we
 
commit
 
ourselves
 
fully
 
to
 
the
 
Church,
 
for
 
it
 
is
 
the
 
hope
 
of
 
the
 
world,
 
reflecting
 
the
 
glory
 
of
 
Christ!
 
133

Believers' Class: Week 33 TOPIC: The Second Coming & Eternal Judgment - Understanding the 
Last
 
Things
 
 (1 Thessalonians 4:16-17, Revelation 20:11-15) Introduction The doctrine of the Second Coming of Jesus Christ and Eternal Judgment 
is
 
central
 
to
 
Christian
 
faith
 
and
 
eschatology.
 
It
 
brings
 
hope,
 
assurance,
 
and
 
a
 
call
 
to
 
live
 
righteously
 
in
 
anticipation
 
of
 
the
 
future
 
fulfillment
 
of
 
God’s
 
promises.
 
Understanding
 
these
 
events
 
through
 
a
 
biblical
 
lens
 
helps
 
believers
 
to
 
live
 
with
 
an
 
eternal
 
perspective,
 
grounded
 
in
 
the
 
reality
 
of
 
both
 
future
 
glory
 
and
 
future
 
judgment.
 As we explore this TOPIC, we will discuss the return of Christ, the final 
judgment,
 
and
 
the
 
distinction
 
between
 
the
 
Judgment
 
Seat
 
of
 
Christ
 
for
 
believers
 
and
 
the
 
White
 
Throne
 
Judgment
 
for
 
unbelievers.
 
As
 
bible
 
students,
 
we
 
recognize
 
that
 
some
 
aspects
 
of
 
biblical
 
prophecy
 
have
 
already
 
been
 
fulfilled
 
in
 
the
 
past,
 
yet
 
the
 
future
 
hope
 
of
 
Christ's
 
return
 
and
 
the
 
eternal
 
judgment
 
remain
 
as
 
certain
 
events
 
that
 
will
 
unfold.
 The Second Coming of Christ - The Hope of the Believer The second coming of Christ is the moment when Jesus returns in glory to 
establish
 
His
 
eternal
 
Kingdom.
 
This
 
is
 
the
 
consummation
 
of
 
God's
 
redemptive
 
plan
 
for
 
humanity.
 
In
 
1
 
Thessalonians
 
4:16-17
,
 
Paul
 
describes
 
this
 
event
 
as
 
the
 
"coming
 
of
 
the
 
Lord"
 
in
 
which
 
He
 
will
 
descend
 
from
 
heaven
 
with
 
a
 
shout,
 
with
 
the
 
voice
 
of
 
the
 
archangel,
 
and
 
with
 
the
 
trumpet
 
of
 
God.
 
At
 
that
 
moment,
 
the
 
dead
 
in
 
Christ
 
will
 
rise
 
first,
 
and
 
those
 
who
 
are
 
alive
 
and
 
remain
 
will
 
be
 
caught
 
up
 
together
 
with
 
them
 
in
 
the
 
clouds
 
to
 
meet
 
the
 
Lord
 
in
 
the
 
air.
 This promise of Christ’s return is not just a future event, it is a living hope 
that
 
sustains
 
believers
 
through
 
the
 
trials
 
and
 
challenges
 
of
 
life.
 
It
 
reminds
 
us
 
that
 
our
 
present
 
struggles
 
are
 
not
 
the
 
end
 
of
 
the
 
story.
 
As
 
we
 
wait
 
for
 
His
 
134

return, we are called to remain faithful, expectant, and diligent in our walk 
with
 
God.
 The White Throne Judgment - A Judgment for Unbelievers In Revelation 20:11-15 , we are given a sobering picture of the White 
Throne
 
Judgment,
 
where
 
all
 
the
 
dead
 
are
 
judged
 
according
 
to
 
their
 
works.
 
This
 
judgment
 
is
 
not
 
for
 
believers
 
but
 
for
 
unbelievers,
 
those
 
who
 
have
 
rejected
 
Christ
 
and
 
His
 
offer
 
of
 
salvation.
 
John
 
describes
 
the
 
scene
 
where
 
the
 
dead,
 
both
 
great
 
and
 
small,
 
stand
 
before
 
God,
 
and
 
books
 
are
 
opened.
 
The
 
ultimate
 
book,
 
the
 
Book
 
of
 
Life
,
 
is
 
also
 
opened.
 
Anyone
 
whose
 
name
 
is
 
not
 
found
 
in
 
the
 
Book
 
of
 
Life
 
is
 
cast
 
into
 
the
 
lake
 
of
 
fire,
 
which
 
is
 
the
 
second
 
death.
 The Book of Life represents Christ Himself, as He is the source of eternal 
life.
 
Therefore,
 
those
 
whose
 
names
 
are
 
not
 
found
 
in
 
the
 
Book
 
of
 
Life
 
are
 
those
 
who
 
have
 
not
 
been
 
reconciled
 
to
 
God
 
through
 
Jesus
 
Christ.
 
It’s
 
not
 
merely
 
a
 
list
 
of
 
names
 
but
 
a
 
representation
 
of
 
one's
 
relationship
 
with
 
Christ.
 
Since
 
those
 
who
 
reject
 
Christ
 
are
 
already
 
condemned
 
(John
 
3:18),
 
this
 
judgment
 
simply
 
confirms
 
their
 
decision
 
to
 
reject
 
the
 
grace
 
and
 
salvation
 
offered
 
through
 
Jesus.
 Key Truths to Remember: ● The White Throne Judgment is not for believers; believers will not 
stand
 
before
 
this
 
judgment.
 ● It is for the unsaved, those who have rejected Christ's salvation. ● Being excluded from the Book of Life signifies rejection of Christ, 
and
 
such
 
individuals
 
face
 
eternal
 
separation
 
from
 
God.
 
 The Judgment Seat of Christ - A Judgment for Believers While the White Throne Judgment is for unbelievers, believers will face a 
different
 
kind
 
of
 
judgment
 
known
 
as
 
the
 
Judgment
 
Seat
 
of
 
Christ
 
(also
 
called
 
the
 
Bema
 
Seat
).
 
This
 
is
 
where
 
believers
 
will
 
give
 
an
 
account
 
of
 
their
 
lives
 
before
 
Christ,
 
not
 
for
 
the
 
purpose
 
of
 
condemnation,
 
but
 
for
 
the
 
135

purpose of reward. 2 Corinthians 5:10 tells us that we must all appear 
before
 
the
 
Judgment
 
Seat
 
of
 
Christ
 
so
 
that
 
each
 
may
 
receive
 
what
 
is
 
due
 
for
 
the
 
things
 
done
 
while
 
in
 
the
 
body,
 
whether
 
good
 
or
 
bad.
 The Judgment Seat of Christ is not about determining salvation, that has 
already
 
been
 
settled
 
by
 
Christ’s
 
sacrifice
 
on
 
the
 
cross,
 
but
 
about
 
rewarding
 
believers
 
for
 
their
 
faithfulness
 
in
 
service.
 
This
 
judgment
 
will
 
be
 
based
 
on
 
how
 
we
 
have
 
lived
 
out
 
our
 
calling,
 
our
 
obedience
 
to
 
God’s
 
Word,
 
and
 
our
 
service
 
in
 
the
 
Kingdom.
 
1
 
Corinthians
 
3:12-15
 
explains
 
that
 
the
 
quality
 
of
 
each
 
person's
 
work
 
will
 
be
 
revealed
 
by
 
fire,
 
and
 
if
 
what
 
they
 
have
 
built
 
survives,
 
they
 
will
 
receive
 
a
 
reward.
 
If
 
not,
 
they
 
will
 
suffer
 
loss,
 
though
 
they
 
themselves
 
will
 
be
 
saved.
 Key Truths to Remember: ● The Judgment Seat of Christ is for believers and will take place 
after
 
the
 
rapture.
 ● It is a judgment of works, not salvation. ● Believers will be rewarded according to their faithfulness in service 
to
 
Christ
 
and
 
His
 
Kingdom.
 
 The Eternal Implications of the Judgment Both judgments, whether the White Throne Judgment or the Judgment Seat 
of
 
Christ,
 
remind
 
us
 
of
 
the
 
eternal
 
significance
 
of
 
our
 
decisions
 
today.
 
The
 
White
 
Throne
 
Judgment
 
warns
 
of
 
the
 
eternal
 
consequences
 
of
 
rejecting
 
Christ,
 
leading
 
to
 
eternal
 
separation
 
from
 
God.
 
It
 
is
 
a
 
call
 
for
 
evangelism
 
and
 
an
 
urgency
 
to
 
share
 
the
 
gospel,
 
for
 
we
 
know
 
that
 
those
 
who
 
do
 
not
 
accept
 
Christ
 
are
 
already
 
condemned.
 On the other hand, the Judgment Seat of Christ encourages believers to live 
lives
 
that
 
honor
 
God,
 
knowing
 
that
 
we
 
will
 
give
 
an
 
account
 
of
 
our
 
lives.
 
It
 
motivates
 
us
 
to
 
serve
 
faithfully,
 
not
 
out
 
of
 
fear,
 
but
 
with
 
the
 
joy
 
of
 
knowing
 
that
 
we
 
will
 
be
 
rewarded
 
for
 
our
 
obedience.
 
136

Devotional Reflection As you reflect on these truths, consider the eternal implications of your 
choices:
 ● Are you living with a sense of urgency to share the gospel, knowing 
that
 
those
 
who
 
do
 
not
 
know
 
Christ
 
are
 
headed
 
for
 
the
 
White
 
Throne
 
Judgment?
 ● Are you living with the hope of the Judgment Seat of Christ, 
knowing
 
that
 
you
 
will
 
be
 
rewarded
 
for
 
your
 
faithfulness?
 ● How does the knowledge of Christ’s return and the eternal 
judgment
 
shape
 
your
 
daily
 
walk
 
with
 
God?
 
 Let us live each day with the awareness that our actions have eternal 
significance.
 
May
 
we
 
be
 
found
 
faithful,
 
eagerly
 
awaiting
 
the
 
return
 
of
 
our
 
Savior.
 Assignment 1. Read Revelation 20:11-15 and write down your understanding of 
the
 
White
 
Throne
 
Judgment.
 2. Read 2 Corinthians 5:10 and reflect on the Judgment Seat of 
Christ.
 
What
 
motivates
 
you
 
to
 
live
 
a
 
life
 
of
 
faithful
 
service?
 3. Memorize 1 Thessalonians 4:16-17 and share with a fellow 
believer
 
the
 
hope
 
of
 
the
 
Second
 
Coming
 
of
 
Christ.
 4. Listen to Pastor’s teachings on Eschatology 
 Conclusion The Second Coming of Christ and the eternal judgment are profound truths 
that
 
shape
 
how
 
we
 
live
 
today.
 
For
 
the
 
believer,
 
it
 
is
 
a
 
reminder
 
to
 
live
 
with
 
a
 
sense
 
of
 
responsibility,
 
knowing
 
that
 
our
 
works
 
will
 
be
 
judged
 
and
 
rewarded.
 
For
 
the
 
unbeliever,
 
it
 
is
 
a
 
call
 
to
 
repentance
 
and
 
faith
 
in
 
Christ,
 
for
 
the
 
White
 
Throne
 
Judgment
 
is
 
a
 
fearful
 
reality.
 
Let
 
us
 
live
 
in
 
light
 
of
 
eternity,
 
keeping
 
our
 
eyes
 
fixed
 
on
 
Jesus,
 
our
 
hope
 
of
 
glory.
 
137

Believers' Class: Week 34 TOPIC: Understanding Grace & Works - The Balance 
 
(Ephesians
 
2:8-10,
 
James
 
2:17)
 Introduction The relationship between grace and works is one of the most significant 
and
 
often
 
misunderstood
 
aspects
 
of
 
the
 
Christian
 
faith.
 
On
 
one
 
hand,
 
we
 
are
 
saved
 
by
 
grace
 
alone,
 
not
 
by
 
anything
 
we
 
can
 
do
 
(Ephesians
 
2:8-9).
 
On
 
the
 
other
 
hand,
 
true
 
faith
 
always
 
results
 
in
 
good
 
works,
 
as
 
we
 
are
 
created
 
in
 
Christ
 
Jesus
 
to
 
do
 
them
 
(Ephesians
 
2:10;
 
James
 
2:17).
 
The
 
tension
 
between
 
these
 
two
 
truths,
 
grace
 
and
 
works,
 
can
 
sometimes
 
confuse
 
believers,
 
but
 
understanding
 
the
 
balance
 
is
 
key
 
to
 
living
 
out
 
the
 
Christian
 
faith
 
effectively.
 Understanding Grace 1. Grace is God’s Unmerited Favor ○ Ephesians 2:8-9 makes it clear that salvation is a gift from 
God.
 
It
 
is
 
not
 
earned
 
or
 
deserved
 
by
 
human
 
effort.
 
Grace
 
is
 
God’s
 
unmerited
 
favor
 
towards
 
us,
 
offered
 
freely,
 
without
 
any
 
strings
 
attached.
 ○ Grace is what enables us to stand before God. It is through 
grace
 
that
 
we
 
are
 
saved,
 
and
 
it
 
is
 
through
 
grace
 
that
 
we
 
are
 
transformed
 
into
 
new
 
creations
 
in
 
Christ
 
(2
 
Corinthians
 
5:17).
 
 2. Grace and Salvation ○ The Bible teaches that salvation is by grace alone, through 
faith
 
alone,
 
in
 
Christ
 
alone.
 
 Ephesians 2:8 says, "For by grace are ye saved through 
faith;
 
and
 
that
 
not
 
of
 
yourselves:
 
it
 
is
 
the
 
gift
 
of
 
God."
 
138

○ Grace frees us from the penalty of sin, but it also empowers 
us
 
to
 
live
 
a
 
godly
 
life.
 
Grace
 
is
 
not
 
just
 
about
 
the
 
initial
 
act
 
of
 
salvation
 
but
 
also
 
about
 
God's
 
ongoing
 
work
 
in
 
our
 
lives,
 
shaping
 
us
 
to
 
reflect
 
Christ.
 
 3. Grace is Empowering ○ Titus 2:11-12 reminds us that the grace of God teaches us to 
live
 
righteous
 
and
 
godly
 
lives.
 
Grace
 
is
 
not
 
a
 
license
 
to
 
sin,
 
but
 
the
 
power
 
to
 
live
 
in
 
a
 
way
 
that
 
honors
 
God.
 ○ The Apostle Paul talks about grace enabling him to do the 
work
 
of
 
ministry:
 
"But
 
by
 
the
 
grace
 
of
 
God
 
I
 
am
 
what
 
I
 
am"
 
(1
 
Corinthians
 
15:10).
 
Grace
 
is
 
the
 
strength
 
to
 
fulfill
 
God’s
 
calling
 
on
 
our
 
lives.
 
 Understanding Works 1. Works Cannot Save Us ○ Ephesians 2:9 clearly tells us that salvation is not by works: 
"Not
 
of
 
works,
 
lest
 
any
 
man
 
should
 
boast."
 
This
 
emphasizes
 
that
 
no
 
human
 
effort,
 
no
 
matter
 
how
 
good,
 
can
 
earn
 
salvation.
 ○ Romans 3:20 and Titus 3:5 affirm this, works, no matter 
how
 
moral
 
or
 
righteous
 
they
 
seem,
 
cannot
 
justify
 
us
 
before
 
God.
 
 2. Works are the Evidence of True Faith ○ While works cannot save us, true faith produces good 
works.
 
James
 
2:17
 
says,
 
"Even
 
so
 
faith,
 
if
 
it
 
hath
 
not
 
works,
 
is
 
dead,
 
being
 
alone."
 
Genuine
 
faith
 
is
 
demonstrated
 
through
 
action.
 
Without
 
works,
 
faith
 
is
 
incomplete.
 ○ Jesus Himself taught that a tree is known by its fruit 
(Matthew
 
7:17-20).
 
In
 
the
 
same
 
way,
 
our
 
lives
 
should
 
reflect
 
the
 
reality
 
of
 
the
 
grace
 
we’ve
 
received,
 
leading
 
to
 
acts
 
of
 
139

service, love, and obedience. 
 3. Works are a Response to Grace ○ Good works are a natural outflow of the grace we’ve 
received.
 
In
 
Ephesians
 
2:10
,
 
we
 
are
 
reminded
 
that
 
we
 
are
 
God’s
 
workmanship,
 
created
 
in
 
Christ
 
Jesus
 
for
 
good
 
works,
 
which
 
God
 
prepared
 
beforehand
 
that
 
we
 
should
 
walk
 
in
 
them.
 ○ Our works are not a means to earn God’s favor, but they are 
a
 
way
 
to
 
show
 
our
 
gratitude
 
for
 
the
 
grace
 
we
 
have
 
received.
 
We
 
serve
 
because
 
we’ve
 
been
 
served;
 
we
 
love
 
because
 
we’ve
 
been
 
loved.
 
 The Balance Between Grace and Works 1. Grace First, Then Works ○ Grace comes first. We are saved by grace, and then, as a 
result
 
of
 
that
 
grace,
 
we
 
are
 
empowered
 
to
 
do
 
good
 
works.
 
Ephesians
 
2:10
 
makes
 
this
 
clear:
 
we
 
were
 
created
 
in
 
Christ
 
Jesus
 
for
 
good
 
works,
 
not
 
by
 
good
 
works.
 ○ James 2:22 shows us how Abraham's faith and works were 
interconnected.
 
His
 
faith
 
was
 
demonstrated
 
by
 
his
 
actions,
 
but
 
his
 
faith
 
preceded
 
his
 
works.
 
In
 
other
 
words,
 
we
 
don't
 
work
 
to
 
get
 
saved;
 
we
 
work
 
because
 
we
 
are
 
saved.
 
 2. Grace Leads to Transformation ○ Grace doesn’t leave us where we are. It changes us. It leads 
us
 
to
 
live
 
differently,
 
in
 
a
 
way
 
that
 
reflects
 
Christ.
 
As
 
Philippians
 
2:13
 
says,
 
"For
 
it
 
is
 
God
 
which
 
worketh
 
in
 
you
 
both
 
to
 
will
 
and
 
to
 
do
 
of
 
his
 
good
 
pleasure."
 
The
 
transformation
 
produced
 
by
 
grace
 
is
 
visible
 
through
 
our
 
works.
 
 3. Works Must Be Done in the Right Heart 
140

○ Matthew 6:1-4 reminds us that works should be done with 
the
 
right
 
heart,
 
not
 
for
 
self-glory,
 
but
 
for
 
God’s
 
glory.
 
The
 
motivation
 
behind
 
our
 
works
 
matters.
 
If
 
we
 
are
 
doing
 
good
 
deeds
 
to
 
be
 
seen
 
by
 
others,
 
we
 
miss
 
the
 
point.
 ○ Our works must flow out of a heart of love for God and 
others.
 
They
 
should
 
reflect
 
the
 
love,
 
grace,
 
and
 
mercy
 
that
 
we
 
have
 
received.
 
 Practical Application 1. Live Out Your Faith ○ In practical terms, apply the grace you’ve received by 
loving
 
others,
 
serving
 
those
 
in
 
need,
 
and
 
living
 
a
 
life
 
of
 
integrity
 
and
 
holiness.
 
Your
 
works
 
should
 
be
 
a
 
reflection
 
of
 
the
 
grace
 
that
 
has
 
changed
 
your
 
heart.
 ○ Take time to examine your own life: Are there areas where 
you’re
 
trying
 
to
 
work
 
for
 
God’s
 
approval,
 
rather
 
than
 
working
 
out
 
of
 
gratitude
 
for
 
the
 
grace
 
He’s
 
already
 
given?
 
 2. Be Active in Good Works ○ Let your faith be active. Involve yourself in the service of 
others.
 
Reach
 
out
 
to
 
the
 
hurting,
 
give
 
generously,
 
and
 
be
 
involved
 
in
 
your
 
local
 
church’s
 
mission.
 ○ Remember that your good works are a testimony of God’s 
love
 
and
 
grace
 
to
 
the
 
world
 
around
 
you.
 
 Devotional Reflection Consider how grace and works are intertwined in your own life. Are you 
relying
 
on
 
grace
 
alone
 
for
 
salvation,
 
or
 
are
 
you
 
also
 
relying
 
on
 
your
 
own
 
works?
 
Remember,
 
works
 
don’t
 
save
 
us,
 
but
 
they
 
demonstrate
 
the
 
life-changing
 
power
 
of
 
grace
 
in
 
our
 
lives.
 
Let
 
us,
 
therefore,
 
be
 
doers
 
of
 
the
 
Word,
 
demonstrating
 
our
 
faith
 
through
 
love
 
and
 
service.
 
141

Assignment 1. Reflect on your life and identify one area where you can live out 
the
 
good
 
works
 
God
 
has
 
prepared
 
for
 
you.
 2. Write down a prayer of thanksgiving, thanking God for His grace, 
and
 
asking
 
Him
 
to
 
help
 
you
 
demonstrate
 
that
 
grace
 
in
 
your
 
actions.
 3. Share with a fellow believer how grace has transformed your life 
and
 
the
 
role
 
of
 
good
 
works
 
in
 
expressing
 
your
 
faith.
 
 Conclusion Understanding the balance between grace and works is essential for the 
Christian
 
life.
 
Grace
 
is
 
the
 
foundation;
 
it
 
is
 
the
 
starting
 
point
 
of
 
our
 
salvation
 
and
 
transformation.
 
Works
 
are
 
the
 
natural
 
outcome
 
of
 
that
 
grace,
 
a
 
demonstration
 
of
 
our
 
faith
 
in
 
action.
 
Let
 
us
 
rest
 
in
 
the
 
grace
 
of
 
God
 
while
 
living
 
out
 
our
 
faith
 
through
 
good
 
works.
 
As
 
we
 
do,
 
we
 
bring
 
glory
 
to
 
God
 
and
 
fulfill
 
His
 
purposes
 
for
 
our
 
lives.
 
142

Believers' Class: Week 35 TOPIC: Spiritual Gifts and Calling 
Discovering
 
and
 
Operating
 
in
 
Gifts
 Introduction Spiritual gifts are divine abilities given by the Holy Spirit to every believer 
for
 
the
 
edification
 
of
 
the
 
body
 
of
 
Christ.
 
Understanding
 
these
 
gifts
 
helps
 
believers
 
function
 
effectively
 
in
 
their
 
calling
 
and
 
contribute
 
to
 
the
 
mission
 
of
 
the
 
Church.
 This study will explore: 1. The Nature of Spiritual Gifts (Romans 12:6-8, 1 Corinthians 
12:4-11)
 2. The Distribution and Operation of Gifts 3. Discovering and Functioning in Spiritual Gifts 4. The Purpose of Spiritual Gifts in the Church 
 1. The Nature of Spiritual Gifts Key Texts: Romans 12:6-8; 1 Corinthians 12:4-11 1. Spiritual gifts are given to every believer 1 Corinthians 12:7 - "But the manifestation of the Spirit is 
given
 
to
 
every
 
man
 
to
 
profit
 
withal."
 
 Every believer possesses spiritual gifts for the benefit of the body of 
Christ.
 
 2. There are different kinds of gifts, but the same Spirit 1 Corinthians 12:4-6 - "Now there are diversities of gifts, 
but
 
the
 
same
 
Spirit.
 
And
 
there
 
are
 
differences
 
of
 
administrations,
 
but
 
the
 
same
 
Lord."
 
 
144

Though believers have different functions, all gifts come from the same 
Holy
 
Spirit.
 
 3. Gifts function by grace and faith Romans 12:6 - "Having then gifts differing according to the 
grace
 
that
 
is
 
given
 
to
 
us,
 
whether
 
prophecy,
 
let
 
us
 
prophesy
 
according
 
to
 
the
 
proportion
 
of
 
faith."
 
 Gifts operate based on grace and require faith to function effectively. 
 2. The Distribution and Operation of Gifts Key Text: 1 Corinthians 12:8-11 A. Every Believer Has Access to Spiritual Gifts ● The Holy Spirit dwells in all believers, meaning every believer has 
access
 
to
 
the
 
gifts.
 1 Corinthians 12:7 - "The manifestation of the Spirit is given to 
every
 
man
 
to
 
profit
 
withal."
 
 B. Do All Believers Have All Gifts? 1 Corinthians 12:8-10 lists gifts such as wisdom, knowledge, faith, 
healing,
 
miracles,
 
prophecy,
 
discernment,
 
tongues,
 
and
 
interpretation.
 "To one is given… to another…" This does not mean some gifts are 
exclusive
 
to
 
certain
 
individuals,
 
but
 
that
 
the
 
Spirit
 
distributes
 
gifts
 
based
 
on
 
the
 
needs
 
of
 
the
 
body
 
at
 
a
 
given
 
time.
 
 1 Corinthians 12:11 - "But all these worketh that one and the 
selfsame
 
Spirit,
 
dividing
 
to
 
every
 
man
 
severally
 
as
 
he
 
will."
 
 ● Balanced View: 
-
 
Every
 
believer
 
has
 
the
 
gifts
 
of
 
the
 
Spirit.
 
145

- The Holy Spirit determines when and how gifts manifest. 
 
-
 
Believers
 
may
 
operate
 
more
 
frequently
 
in
 
certain
 
gifts,
 
but
 
no
 
gift
 
is
 
permanently
 
exclusive
 
to
 
an
 
individual.
 
However,
 
those
 
in
 
the
 
fivefold
 
ministry
 
require
 
specific
 
gifts
 
to
 
effectively
 
fulfill
 
their
 
calling.
 
 3. Discovering and Functioning in Spiritual Gifts A. Desire and Yielding to the Holy Spirit 1 Corinthians 14:1 - "Follow after charity, and desire spiritual gifts, 
but
 
rather
 
that
 
ye
 
may
 
prophesy."
 
 Believers are encouraged to desire spiritual gifts, especially those that 
edify
 
the
 
Church.
 
 2 Timothy 1:6 - "Wherefore I put thee in remembrance that thou stir 
up
 
the
 
gift
 
of
 
God,
 
which
 
is
 
in
 
thee
 
by
 
the
 
putting
 
on
 
of
 
my
 
hands."
 
 Gifts must be stirred up through faith and practice. 
 B. Identifying Spiritual Gifts ● By Function in the Body ○ Romans 12:6-8 outlines gifts such as teaching, exhortation, 
giving,
 
leadership,
 
and
 
mercy.
 ○ A believer can identify their gifts by recognizing how God 
uses
 
them
 
to
 
serve
 
others.
 
 ● By the Leading of the Holy Spirit ○ The Spirit often reveals gifts during times of prayer, service, 
and
 
fellowship.
 
 4. The Purpose of Spiritual Gifts in the Church 
146

Key Text: 1 Corinthians 14:26 1. Gifts Are for Edification 1 Corinthians 12:7 - "The manifestation of the Spirit is 
given
 
to
 
every
 
man
 
to
 
profit
 
withal."
 
 Gifts benefit the entire church, not just the individual. 
 2. Gifts Function in Church Gatherings 
 1 Corinthians 14:26 - "How is it then, brethren? when ye 
come
 
together,
 
every
 
one
 
of
 
you
 
hath
 
a
 
psalm,
 
hath
 
a
 
doctrine,
 
hath
 
a
 
tongue,
 
hath
 
a
 
revelation,
 
hath
 
an
 
interpretation.
 
Let
 
all
 
things
 
be
 
done
 
unto
 
edifying."
 
 Gifts manifest as needed to build up the body. 
 Conclusion: ● Spiritual gifts are given to every believer. ● The Spirit determines how and when gifts operate. ● Every believer has access to all gifts, but some may function more 
frequently
 
in
 
certain
 
gifts.
 ● Gifts are for the edification of the Church and should be stirred up 
by
 
faith.
 
 Application Questions: 1. What gifts do you recognize operating in your life? 2. How can you stir up the gifts God has given you? 3. How can you use your gifts to edify the body of Christ? 
 Memory Verse: 
 
1
 
Corinthians
 
12:7
 
-
 
"But
 
the
 
manifestation
 
of
 
the
 
Spirit
 
is
 
given
 
to
 
every
 
man
 
to
 
profit
 
withal."
 
147

Prayer: 
 
Lord,
 
help
 
me
 
to
 
recognize
 
and
 
stir
 
up
 
the
 
gifts
 
You
 
have
 
placed
 
in
 
me.
 
Let
 
me
 
be
 
a
 
vessel
 
for
 
Your
 
work
 
and
 
a
 
blessing
 
to
 
the
 
Church.
 
Amen.

Believers' Class: Week 36 The Power of Fasting - Spiritual Benefits of Fasting Key Texts: Isaiah 58:6-8, Matthew 6:16-18 Introduction Fasting is a powerful spiritual discipline that brings believers into deeper 
intimacy
 
with
 
God.
 
It
 
is
 
not
 
merely
 
abstaining
 
from
 
food
 
but
 
a
 
deliberate
 
act
 
of
 
seeking
 
God
 
with
 
sincerity
 
and
 
humility.
 
Throughout
 
Scripture,
 
fasting
 
is
 
presented
 
as
 
a
 
means
 
to
 
experience
 
spiritual
 
breakthroughs,
 
divine
 
direction,
 
and
 
increased
 
intimacy
 
with
 
God.
 This study will explore: 1. The Biblical Purpose of Fasting 2. The Spiritual Benefits of Fasting 3. The Proper Attitude in Fasting 4. How to Fast Effectively 
 1. The Biblical Purpose of Fasting Key Text: Isaiah 58:6 - "Is not this the fast that I have chosen? to loose the 
bands
 
of
 
wickedness,
 
to
 
undo
 
the
 
heavy
 
burdens,
 
and
 
to
 
let
 
the
 
oppressed
 
go
 
free,
 
and
 
that
 
ye
 
break
 
every
 
yoke?"
 Fasting serves several divine purposes: ● Spiritual Renewal: It brings a believer into a state of spiritual 
sensitivity
 
and
 
deeper
 
connection
 
with
 
God.
 ● Deliverance: It breaks chains of oppression and spiritual bondage. ● Repentance and Humility: It demonstrates humility before God 
and
 
genuine
 
repentance.
 ● Divine Intervention: It opens doors for God’s supernatural help in 
challenging
 
situations

4:16,

● Empowerment for Ministry: Jesus fasted before starting His

voice
 
more

4:1-11).

(Matthew
 
17:21).
 ● Many biblical figures fasted for divine intervention (Ezra 8:21-23, 
Nehemiah
 
1:4).

● Fasting enhances spiritual authority and power, as seen in Jesus’ 
ministry
 
(Luke
 
4:14).
 ● The early church fasted before making major decisions (Acts 
13:2-3).
 
 3. The Proper Attitude in Fasting Key Text: Matthew 6:16-18 - "Moreover when ye fast, be not, as the 
hypocrites,
 
of
 
a
 
sad
 
countenance:

they
 
disfigure

unto
 
men

unto

They

(Matthew
 
6:17-18).

religious
 
routine
 
(Zechariah
 
7:5-6).

will.
 
 4. How to Fast Effectively A. Prepare Spiritually and Physically ● Set a clear purpose for fasting. ● Reduce distractions and dedicate time to seeking God. 
 B. Choose the Right Type of Fast 151

● Absolute Fast: No food or water (Esther 4:16, Acts 9:9). ● Normal Fast: Only water (Matthew 4:2). ● Partial Fast: Abstaining from certain foods (Daniel 10:3). ● Intermittent Fast: Specific fasting periods (Luke 18:12). 
 C. Engage in Prayer and Worship ● Set specific prayer times during the fast. ● Meditate on the Word and listen for God’s direction. 
 D. Break the Fast Wisely ● Avoid overeating when concluding a fast. ● Reintroduce food gradually to avoid digestive issues.

with
 
God's
 
will.

breakthroughs,

and
 
greater
 
sensitivity
 
to
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit.

the

a

small

of

there
 
is
 
safety."

great
 
and
 
mighty
 
things,
 
which
 
thou
 
knowest
 
not."

 B. Meditate on God’s Word Psalm 119:11 - "Thy word have I hid in mine heart, that I might not

against
 
thee."

 C. Cultivate a Heart of Obedience John 7:17 - "If any man will do his will, he shall know of the 
doctrine."

A willingness to obey sharpens our ability to discern God’s direction. 
 D. Practice Spiritual Stillness Psalm 46:10 - "Be still, and know that I am God."

 E. Develop Spiritual Maturity Hebrews 5:14 - "But strong meat belongeth to them that are of full

of
 
use

senses

 4. Obedience: The Key to Walking in God’s Will A. Obedience Unlocks More Revelation Psalm 25:9 - "The meek will he guide in judgment: and the meek

worker

.

importance
 
of

and

before

fall."

and
 
accountability.

D. Practicing Humility in Ministry ● Acknowledge that your role is to serve and glorify God, not

3:6-7).
 
 2. Faithfulness: The Mark of a True Servant Key Texts: 1 Timothy 3:8-10, Matthew 25:21 A. Definition of Faithfulness ● Faithfulness means being reliable, trustworthy, and consistent in 
one’s
 
commitments.
 1 Corinthians 4:2 - "Moreover it is required in stewards, that a man 
be
 
found
 
faithful."

in
 
much."

diligent
 
in
 
small
 
tasks.

able
 
by
 
sound
 
doctrine

to

and
 
to
 
convince
 
the
 
gainsayers."

biblical
 
values.

(James

over

few
 
things,

make
 
thee
 
ruler
 
over
 
many
 
things."
 
 E. Practical Ways to Demonstrate Faithfulness ● Keep commitments and honor your word. ● Serve diligently even when no one is watching. ● Be punctual, prepared, and responsible in ministry duties. ● Stay devoted to prayer and the Word. 
 3. Combining Humility and Faithfulness in Christian Service A. Humility makes service pure, while faithfulness ensures consistency. B. A Christian worker must be humble enough to serve and faithful

 C. Both virtues help build a strong and lasting ministry. Conclusion A Christian worker must exemplify humility, recognizing that their

come

in

faithful

25:21)

Believers' Class: Week 44 TOPIC: Christian Leadership - Servant Leadership Key Texts: Mark 10:42-45 Introduction Christian leadership is distinct from secular leadership in that it is built 
upon
 
the
 
foundation
 
of
 
servanthood
 
rather
 
than
 
dominance
 
or
 
authority.
 
In
 
Mark
 
10:42-45,
 
Jesus
 
teaches
 
His
 
disciples
 
that
 
the
 
essence
 
of
 
true
 
leadership
 
is
 
found
 
in
 
serving
 
others.
 
This
 
lesson
 
is
 
a
 
crucial
 
one,
 
especially
 
in
 
a
 
world
 
where
 
leadership
 
often
 
means
 
power,
 
control,
 
and
 
position.
 
Jesus,
 
the
 
greatest
 
leader,
 
turned
 
that
 
concept
 
upside
 
down
 
by
 
modeling
 
leadership
 
through
 
service
 
and
 
humility.
 In this lesson, we will explore: 1. The Definition of Servant Leadership 2. Characteristics of a Servant Leader 3. The Model of Jesus’ Leadership 4. The Impact of Servant Leadership 
 1. The Definition of Servant Leadership Key Text: Mark 10:42-43 
 "But Jesus called them to him, and saith unto them, Ye know that 
they
 
which
 
are
 
accounted
 
to
 
rule
 
over
 
the
 
Gentiles
 
exercise
 
lordship
 
over
 
them;
 
and
 
their
 
great
 
ones
 
exercise
 
authority
 
upon
 
them.
 
But
 
so
 
shall
 
it
 
not
 
be
 
among
 
you:
 
but
 
whosoever
 
will
 
be
 
great
 
among
 
you
 
shall
 
be
 
your
 
minister."
 Servant leadership is the model in which leaders prioritize the needs of 
others
 
over
 
their
 
own.
 
Rather
 
than
 
seeking
 
personal
 
gain
 
or
 
power,
 
a
 
servant
 
leader
 
seeks
 
to
 
serve,
 
empower,
 
and
 
elevate
 
those
 
they
 
lead.
 ● Servant Leadership focuses on the growth and well-being of 
people
 
and
 
the
 
communities
 
to
 
which
 
they
 
belong.
 
185

● A servant leader uses their influence to serve others, rather than 
seeking
 
to
 
be
 
served.
 ● Jesus’ definition of leadership is not about asserting control or 
dominating
 
others,
 
but
 
about
 
becoming
 
a
 
servant
 
to
 
all.
 
 Application: ● Leadership in the Christian context is about serving, not lording 
over
 
others.
 ● In our leadership roles, we must focus on meeting the needs of 
others
 
and
 
empowering
 
them
 
to
 
succeed.
 
 2. Characteristics of a Servant Leader Key Text: Mark 10:44 
 "And whosoever of you will be the chiefest, shall be servant of 
all."
 Jesus gives us a clear directive here: the greatest among you is the one who 
serves.
 
The
 
characteristics
 
of
 
a
 
servant
 
leader
 
include:
 A. Humility ● A servant leader does not seek status or honor, but humbly serves 
others.
 ● Humility is at the core of Christian leadership. Jesus Himself, 
though
 
the
 
Son
 
of
 
God,
 
demonstrated
 
unparalleled
 
humility
 
by
 
serving
 
His
 
disciples.
 
 B. Selflessness ● A servant leader puts the needs of others before their own. ● A true servant leader doesn’t consider their own advancement but 
focuses
 
on
 
the
 
good
 
of
 
those
 
they
 
lead.
 
 C. Sacrificial Love ● The heart of servanthood is sacrificial love. 
186

● Jesus exemplified this ultimate sacrifice by giving His life for the 
redemption
 
of
 
humanity.
 
Leadership
 
in
 
Christ’s
 
kingdom
 
requires
 
sacrificial
 
love,
 
putting
 
others
 
first
 
even
 
at
 
great
 
personal
 
cost.
 
 D. Empowering Others ● A servant leader equips others to grow, take responsibility, and 
succeed.
 ● They are not threatened by the success of those they lead, but 
rather,
 
they
 
rejoice
 
in
 
seeing
 
others
 
grow
 
and
 
flourish.
 
 3. The Model of Jesus’ Leadership Key Text: Mark 10:45 
 "For even the Son of man came not to be ministered unto, but to 
minister,
 
and
 
to
 
give
 
his
 
life
 
a
 
ransom
 
for
 
many."
 Jesus is the ultimate model of servant leadership. He did not come to be 
served
 
but
 
to
 
serve.
 
His
 
life
 
exemplified
 
selflessness,
 
humility,
 
and
 
sacrifice.
 
Throughout
 
His
 
ministry,
 
Jesus
 
served
 
by
 
healing
 
the
 
sick,
 
teaching,
 
and
 
ultimately
 
laying
 
down
 
His
 
life
 
for
 
others.
 A. Jesus Was the Ultimate Servant Leader ● He washed His disciples' feet, a task reserved for the lowliest 
servant
 
in
 
the
 
house
 
(John
 
13:1-17).
 ● He lived a life of service and sacrifice, never seeking glory for 
Himself
 
but
 
always
 
pointing
 
to
 
the
 
Father.
 
 B. Jesus’ Leadership Was Marked by Compassion ● Jesus demonstrated deep compassion for those He served, whether 
it
 
was
 
the
 
hungry
 
crowds
 
or
 
the
 
outcast
 
sinners.
 ● Compassion for others is a vital component of servant leadership. 
 Application: 
187

● As leaders, we must be willing to serve others, even in ways that 
may
 
be
 
uncomfortable
 
or
 
seem
 
beneath
 
us.
 ● Leadership is not about position, but about sacrificial service and 
love.
 
 4. The Impact of Servant Leadership Key Text: Mark 10:45 (Continued) 
 
"…and
 
to
 
give
 
his
 
life
 
a
 
ransom
 
for
 
many."
 A. Transformation of Others ● Servant leadership has the power to transform lives. When leaders 
serve
 
others,
 
they
 
help
 
develop
 
the
 
leadership
 
potential
 
in
 
others.
 ● Jesus’ model of leadership created a movement that changed the 
world.
 
 B. Building Community ● Servant leadership fosters unity, love, and mutual respect. When 
leaders
 
prioritize
 
others,
 
they
 
create
 
an
 
environment
 
where
 
everyone
 
thrives.
 ● By serving others, leaders build a community of trust, support, and 
mutual
 
care.
 
 C. Advancing the Kingdom ● Servant leadership is central to advancing the kingdom of God. 
Jesus
 
came
 
to
 
serve,
 
and
 
through
 
His
 
servanthood,
 
He
 
redeemed
 
humanity.
 ● As leaders, we follow in His footsteps, and our service points 
others
 
to
 
Christ
 
and
 
advances
 
His
 
purposes
 
on
 
earth.
 
 Conclusion: The Call to Servant Leadership True leadership in the Christian context is rooted in servanthood. Jesus’ 
teaching
 
in
 
Mark
 
10:42-45
 
challenges
 
us
 
to
 
lay
 
aside
 
any
 
desire
 
for
 
power
 
or
 
position
 
and
 
to
 
focus
 
on
 
serving
 
others
 
with
 
humility,
 
love,
 
and
 
sacrifice.
 
188

When we serve in this way, we reflect the heart of Christ and build up the 
body
 
of
 
Christ.
 Application Questions: 1. What are some ways you can demonstrate servant leadership in 
your
 
life
 
and
 
ministry?
 2. How can you put the needs of others ahead of your own in your 
leadership
 
role?
 3. In what areas can you grow in humility and sacrificial love as a 
leader?

of
 
all."
 Prayer: Lord, thank You for the example of servant leadership that Jesus set for us. 
Help
 
me
 
to
 
walk
 
in
 
humility,
 
selflessness,
 
and
 
sacrificial
 
love
 
as
 
I
 
serve
 
others.
 
Teach
 
me
 
to
 
lead
 
as
 
You
 
led
 
-
 
with
 
a
 
heart
 
to
 
serve,
 
empower,
 
and
 
build
 
up
 
others.
 
May
 
my
 
leadership
 
bring
 
glory
 
to
 
You
 
and
 
bless
 
those
 
I
 
lead.
 
In
 
Jesus'
 
name,

Believers' Class: Week 47 Overcoming Burnout in Ministry - Spiritual Renewal Key Text: Isaiah 40:31 Introduction Ministry can be a rewarding yet demanding endeavor, often leading to 
exhaustion
 
and
 
burnout.
 
Serving
 
God
 
and
 
others
 
with

is

without

weary
 
and
 
overwhelmed.

Isaiah

promises

divine

through

power
 
and
 
presence.
 
This
 
promise

us
 
that
 
when
 
we
 
rely
 
on

He
 
will

and
 
help
 
us
 
to

serving

this
 
session,
 
we
 
will
 
explore
 
how
 
to
 
overcome
 
burnout
 
and
 
experience
 
spiritual
 
renewal
 
in
 
ministry.
 1. Understanding Burnout in Ministry What is burnout? 
 
Burnout
 
is
 
a
 
state
 
of
 
emotional,
 
physical,
 
and
 
spiritual
 
exhaustion
 
caused
 
by
 
prolonged
 
stress,
 
overwork,
 
and
 
lack
 
of
 
rest.
 
In
 
ministry,
 
burnout
 
can
 
be
 
particularly
 
detrimental
 
as
 
it
 
affects
 
both
 
the
 
minister's
 
spiritual
 
life
 
and
 
their
 
ability
 
to
 
serve
 
others
 
effectively.
 Common causes of burnout in ministry include: ● Excessive workload : Too many responsibilities and demands can 
overwhelm
 
a
 
minister.
 ● Lack of rest : Neglecting to take time off or recharge can lead to 
exhaustion.
 ● Isolation : Ministers can sometimes feel alone in their work, leading 
to
 
feelings
 
of
 
isolation
 
or
 
loneliness.
 ● Unresolved emotional stress : Unaddressed personal or 
ministry-related
 
issues
 
can
 
take
 
a
 
toll
 
on
 
emotional
 
and
 
spiritual
 
well-being.
 
200

● Unrealistic expectations : Ministers may set high expectations for 
themselves
 
or
 
feel
 
pressure
 
from
 
others,
 
leading
 
to
 
frustration
 
and
 
burnout.
 
 The impact of burnout : ● Decreased spiritual vitality ● Emotional and physical exhaustion ● Loss of passion for ministry ● Spiritual dryness ● Difficulty hearing God's voice ● Neglect of personal well-being 
 Application: ● Reflect on the causes of burnout in your own life. Are you 
experiencing
 
any
 
of
 
these
 
factors?
 ● Consider how burnout has affected your ability to serve and grow 
spiritually.

ministry.

 d) Delegate and Share the Load In ministry, it’s easy to take on too many responsibilities, believing that

depends
 
on

others

and

of

not
 
need
 
to
 
do
 
everything
 
yourself.

others
 
to

on

 4. The Power of Spiritual Renewal Spiritual renewal is not a one-time event but a continuous process that 
strengthens
 
us
 
for
 
the
 
long

As

wait

Believers' Class: Week 48 Preaching & Teaching Effectively - Preparing Sermons/Bible Studies Key Text: 2 Timothy 4:2, 2 Timothy 2:15 Introduction Preaching and teaching are foundational to the growth of the church and 
the
 
spiritual
 
maturity
 
of
 
believers.

communication

with

longsuffering
 
and

Preaching
 
and

emphasizes

to

 2. Preparing a Biblical Sermon/Bible Study a) Begin with Prayer and Seek God’s Guidance The foundation of any effective sermon or Bible study is prayer. You must 
seek
 
God’s
 
guidance,
 
asking
 
for
 
clarity,
 
wisdom,
 
and
 
revelation
 
as

prepare.
 
The
 
Holy
 
Spirit
 
is
 
the
 
true
 
teacher,
 
and
 
without

help,

efforts

● Pray for insight : Ask God to open your heart and mind to the 
Scriptures.

for
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit
 
to

the
 
passage
 
you
 
are
 
studying
 
and
 
to
 
guide
 
your
 
understanding.
 ● Pray for your audience : Ask God to prepare the hearts of those 
who
 
will
 
hear
 
the
 
message,
 
that

may
 
receive
 
it
 
with
 
understanding
 
and
 
apply
 
it
 
in
 
their
 
lives.

invite
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit
 
into
 
the
 
process.
 ● How can you make prayer a priority in your sermon preparation? 
 b) Choose a Passage and Focus on Context Selecting the right passage is critical to preparing a biblical sermon or 
study.
 
Ensure
 
that
 
the
 
passage
 
you
 
choose
 
aligns
 
with
 
the
 
needs
 
of
 
your
 
congregation
 
and
 
the
 
message
 
God
 
wants
 
to
 
communicate.
 
After
 
selecting
 
a
 
passage,
 
study
 
it
 
thoroughly.
 ● Context is crucial : Understand the historical and cultural

of
 
the
 
passage.
