was
 
it
 
written?
 
What
 
was
 
the
 
author’s
 
intent?
 
What
 
was
 
the
 
audience’s
 
context?
 ● Interpretation : Use proper hermeneutical methods to interpret the 
Scripture.
 
Make
 
sure
 
to
 
interpret
 
the
 
passage
 
within
 
its
 
immediate
 
context
 
and
 
the
 
broader
 
context

the
 
Bible.

that

 c) Define the Main Message (Theme) Every sermon or Bible study must have a clear central message or theme. 
The
 
theme
 
should
 
be
 
the
 
focus
 
of
 
the
 
entire
 
message,
 
and
 
every
 
point
 209

should contribute to it. A clear theme helps the listener grasp the main 
takeaway
 
from
 
the
 
teaching.
 ● What is God saying? : Identify the central truth in the passage. 
This
 
is
 
the
 
message
 
God
 
wants
 
to
 
communicate
 
to
 
His
 
people.
 ● Be clear and concise : Avoid tangential ideas that distract from the 
main
 
theme.
 
Stay
 
focused
 
on
 
the
 
purpose
 
of
 
the
 
message.
 
 Application: ● What is the central message of the last sermon you prepared? ● How can you ensure that each teaching is clear and focused? 
 d) Develop the Outline and Structure Once you have a clear theme, begin to outline the sermon or Bible study. A 
well-organized
 
structure
 
will
 
make
 
your
 
message
 
more
 
understandable
 
and
 
impactful.
 
Good
 
structure
 
is
 
essential
 
for
 
effective
 
communication.
 ● Introduction : Start with an engaging introduction that captures 
attention.
 
It
 
should
 
present
 
the
 
problem
 
or
 
need
 
that
 
the
 
Scripture
 
addresses.
 ● Body : Break the sermon into several main points, each supporting 
the
 
central
 
theme.
 
Use
 
Scripture
 
to
 
back
 
up
 
each
 
point,
 
and
 
make
 
sure
 
each
 
point
 
flows
 
logically
 
from
 
one
 
to
 
the
 
next.
 ● Conclusion : Wrap up the message by summarizing the main points 
and
 
offering
 
a
 
clear
 
application
 
for
 
the
 
audience.
 
Challenge
 
them
 
to
 
respond
 
to
 
the
 
message.
 
 Application: ● How do you structure your sermons and Bible studies? ● Consider your last sermon: did it have a clear introduction, body, 
and
 
conclusion?
 
 e) Use Illustrations and Practical Applications 
210

Illustrations make abstract truths more tangible and help the audience 
connect
 
emotionally
 
with
 
the
 
message.
 
Use
 
relevant
 
stories,
 
real-life
 
examples,
 
and
 
analogies
 
to
 
illustrate
 
biblical
 
truths.

 ● Practical application : Make sure that the teaching is applicable to 
everyday
 
life.
 
Show
 
how
 
the
 
biblical
 
truth
 
can
 
be
 
lived
 
out
 
by
 
your
 
audience.
 
 Application: ● How can you use illustrations and stories in your sermons to make 
them
 
more
 
engaging
 
and
 
relatable?
 ● How can you ensure that your message has a practical application 
for
 
your
 
audience?
 
 f) Allow Time for Reflection and Response Effective preaching and teaching lead to transformation. After delivering 
the
 
message,

through

to

 ● Invitation to respond : Give your audience an opportunity to 
respond
 
to
 
the
 
Word.
 
This
 
can
 
include
 
a
 
challenge,
 
a
 
prayer,
 
or
 
a
 
moment
 
of
 
introspection.
 ● Follow-up : Offer resources or next steps for those who want to 
dive
 
deeper
 
into
 
the
 
TOPIC.

your
 
teaching?
 ● How do you encourage your audience to apply what they’ve 
learned?

daily

 Application: ● How can you ensure that the guidance you provide is based on 
biblical
 
truth?
 ● Reflect on a situation where you applied Scripture to counsel 
someone.
 
How
 
did
 
it
 
impact
 
them?
 
 218

4. Practical Steps in Providing Pastoral Care and Counseling a) Create a Safe and Confidential Environment Believers seeking counsel need to know that their struggles are being 
handled
 
with
 
care
 
and
 
confidentiality.
 
A
 
safe
 
environment
 
is
 
one
 
where
 
they
 
can
 
open
 
up
 
without
 
fear
 
of
 
judgment
 
or
 
gossip.
 ● Maintain confidentiality : Keep the information shared in 
counseling
 
sessions
 
confidential
 
unless
 
there
 
is
 
a
 
need
 
for
 
intervention
 
(e.g.,
 
safety
 
concerns).
 ● Establish trust : Build a relationship of trust where individuals feel 
comfortable
 
sharing
 
their
 
struggles.
 
 Application: ● How can you establish a safe and confidential space for counseling 
in
 
your
 
ministry?
 ● Have you encountered any challenges in maintaining 
confidentiality,
 
and
 
how
 
can
 
you
 
address
 
them?
 
 b) Use Discretion When Giving Counsel Not all situations require the same response. It is important to discern the 
best
 
course
 
of
 
action
 
in
 
each
 
situation,
 
whether
 
that
 
involves
 
offering
 
prayer,
 
recommending
 
professional
 
help,
 
or
 
providing
 
biblical
 
counsel.
 ● Know when to refer : If the situation requires specialized 
counseling,
 
such
 
as
 
mental
 
health
 
support,
 
be
 
willing
 
to
 
refer
 
individuals
 
to
 
professional
 
counselors.
 ● Be sensitive : Be aware of the person’s emotional state and needs, 
adjusting
 
your
 
approach
 
accordingly.
 
 Application: ● How do you determine when to offer counsel and when to refer 
someone
 
for
 
professional
 
help?
 ● Reflect on how you can be more discerning in your approach to 
pastoral
 
care.
 
 219

5. Avoiding Burnout in Pastoral Counseling Counseling can be emotionally and spiritually draining, especially when 
dealing
 
with
 
intense
 
issues
 
or
 
difficult
 
situations.
 
It
 
is
 
important
 
for
 
counselors
 
to
 
maintain
 
their
 
own
 
spiritual
 
and
 
emotional
 
well-being
 
to
 
serve
 
effectively.
 ● Take time for self-care : Regularly engage in personal reflection, 
prayer,
 
and
 
rest.
 ● Seek support : Connect with other ministers for encouragement and 
accountability.
 ● Maintain healthy boundaries : Avoid becoming too emotionally 
involved
 
in
 
the
 
person’s
 
struggles.
 
 Application: ● How do you maintain your own well-being while counseling 
others?
 ● What steps can you take to prevent burnout in your ministry? 
 Conclusion Counseling and pastoral care are vital aspects of ministry, requiring 
empathy,
 
wisdom,
 
and
 
a
 
deep
 
reliance
 
on
 
God’s
 
guidance.
 
As
 
counselors,
 
our
 
role
 
is
 
not
 
to
 
solve
 
problems
 
but
 
to
 
walk
 
alongside
 
believers,
 
pointing
 
them
 
to
 
the
 
healing
 
power
 
of
 
Christ.
 
By
 
offering
 
biblical
 
counsel,
 
prayer,
 
and
 
practical
 
support,
 
we
 
can
 
help
 
others
 
navigate
 
their
 
struggles
 
and
 
grow
 
in
 
their
 
faith.
 
Remember,
 
pastoral
 
care
 
is
 
a
 
reflection
 
of
 
God’s
 
love
 
for
 
His
 
people,
 
and
 
our
 
role
 
is
 
to
 
embody
 
that
 
love
 
as
 
we
 
support
 
and
 
guide
 
others.
 Application Questions: 1. How can you demonstrate Christ’s love and compassion in your 
counseling
 
ministry?

 3. How can you create a safe and confidential environment for those

pastoral
 
care?

4. What steps can you take to ensure that your own emotional and

well-being
 
is
 
maintained
 
as
 
you
 
care
 
for
 
others?

Believers' Class: Week 50 TOPIC: Missionary Work & Outreach - Understanding the Great 
Commission
 Key Texts: Matthew 28:18-20, Acts 1:8, Mark 16:15 Introduction The Great Commission is one of the most central and urgent calls given by 
Jesus
 
to
 
His
 
followers.
 
It
 
is
 
a
 
mandate
 
to
 
go
 
into
 
all
 
the
 
world,
 
preach
 
the
 
gospel,
 
and
 
make
 
disciples
 
of
 
all
 
nations.
 
This
 
week,
 
we
 
will
 
dive
 
into
 
the
 
biblical
 
understanding
 
of
 
missionary
 
work
 
and
 
outreach,
 
exploring
 
its
 
importance,
 
scope,
 
and
 
practical
 
application.
 
As
 
believers,
 
we
 
are
 
called
 
not
 
only
 
to
 
be
 
witnesses
 
of
 
the
 
gospel
 
but
 
also
 
to
 
actively
 
participate
 
in
 
spreading
 
the
 
message
 
of
 
salvation
 
to
 
the
 
ends
 
of
 
the
 
earth.
 
This
 
teaching
 
will
 
explore
 
the
 
heart
 
of
 
missionary
 
work
 
and
 
how
 
every
 
Christian
 
can
 
engage
 
in
 
this
 
divine
 
mission.
 1. The Great Commission - A Clear Command Key Text: Matthew 28:18-20 - "And Jesus came and spake unto them, saying, 
All
 
power
 
is
 
given
 
unto
 
me
 
in
 
heaven
 
and
 
in
 
earth.
 
Go
 
ye
 
therefore,
 
and
 
teach
 
all
 
nations,
 
baptizing
 
them
 
in
 
the
 
name
 
of
 
the
 
Father,
 
and
 
of
 
the
 
Son,
 
and
 
of
 
the
 
Holy
 
Ghost:
 
Teaching
 
them
 
to
 
observe
 
all
 
things
 
whatsoever
 
I
 
have
 
commanded
 
you:
 
and,
 
lo,
 
I
 
am
 
with
 
you
 
alway,
 
even
 
unto
 
the
 
end
 
of
 
the
 
world."
 The Great Commission is the command Jesus gave to His disciples after 
His
 
resurrection.
 
He
 
emphasized
 
that
 
all
 
authority
 
in
 
heaven
 
and
 
on
 
earth
 
had
 
been
 
given
 
to
 
Him,
 
and
 
therefore,
 
He
 
had
 
the
 
right
 
and
 
power
 
to
 
send
 
His
 
followers
 
on
 
this
 
mission.
 
The
 
task
 
is
 
clear:
 
Go,
 
teach,
 
baptize,
 
and
 
make
 
disciples.
 ● Go : The first step is action. The commission requires movement, 
both
 
physically
 
and
 
spiritually.
 
This
 
could
 
mean
 
going
 
to
 
distant
 
lands
 
or
 
simply
 
going
 
next
 
door.
 
222

● Teach : It’s not just about preaching; it’s about making disciples, 
teaching
 
them
 
to
 
live
 
out
 
the
 
teachings
 
of
 
Christ.
 ● Baptize : Baptism is the outward sign of an inward change. It 
signifies
 
the
 
beginning
 
of
 
a
 
new
 
life
 
in
 
Christ.
 
Teach
 
them
 
to
 
obey
:
 
Discipleship
 
involves
 
more
 
than
 
just
 
knowledge;
 
it
 
involves
 
obedience
 
to
 
Jesus’
 
teachings.
 
 Application: ● How can you actively participate in fulfilling the Great 
Commission
 
in
 
your
 
daily
 
life?
 ● Reflect on how the Great Commission influences your 
understanding
 
of
 
your
 
mission
 
in
 
life.
 
 2. The Scope of the Great Commission Key Texts: Acts 1:8 - "But ye shall receive power, after that the Holy Ghost is 
come
 
upon
 
you:
 
and
 
ye
 
shall
 
be
 
witnesses
 
unto
 
me
 
both
 
in
 
Jerusalem,
 
and
 
in
 
all
 
Judaea,
 
and
 
in
 
Samaria,
 
and
 
unto
 
the
 
uttermost
 
part
 
of
 
the
 
earth."
 
 
Mark
 
16:15
 
-
 
"And
 
he
 
said
 
unto
 
them,
 
Go
 
ye
 
into
 
all
 
the
 
world,
 
and
 
preach
 
the
 
gospel
 
to

creature."
 The Great Commission’s scope is global. Jesus’ command is not limited to

group
 
of

area.

to

in

to

and

ends

the
 
earth.
 
This
 
suggests
 
a
 
model
 
for

that

 ● Jerusalem : Start where you are. Outreach begins in our immediate

our
 
communities,
 
and
 
those
 
who
 
are
 
close
 
to

to

out
 
to

for

kingdom.

Christ

the

.

the
 
gospel?
 
 5. Apologetics in Action - Practical Steps for Defending the Faith ● Know the gospel : Before we can defend our faith, we must first 
know
 
what
 
we
 
believe
 
and
 
why
 
we
 
believe
 
it.
 
Take
 
time
 
to
 
study
 
the
 
core
 
doctrines
 
of
 
the
 
Christian
 
faith,
 
such
 
as
 
the
 
nature
 
of
 
God,
 
the
 
person
 
and
 
work
 
of
 
Jesus,
 
salvation,
 
and
 
the
 
authority
 
of
 
Scripture.
 ● Understand common objections : Be aware of common challenges 
and
 
objections
 
to
 
the
 
Christian
 
faith,
 
such
 
as
 
questions
 
about
 
the
 
existence
 
of
 
God,
 
the
 
problem
 
of
 
evil,
 
and
 
the
 
reliability
 
of
 
the
 
Bible.
 
Equip
 
yourself
 
with
 
biblical
 
answers
 
to
 
these
 
objections.
 ● Be patient and gentle : Apologetics is not about arguing to win. It’s 
about
 
explaining
 
the
 
truth
 
with
 
patience
 
and
 
gentleness.
 
Our
 
goal
 
should
 
always
 
be
 
to
 
point
 
others
 
to
 
Christ,
 
not
 
to
 
win
 
an
 
intellectual
 
battle.
 ● Live out the gospel : Our lives are the most powerful apologetic. 
When
 
people
 
see
 
the
 
love,
 
peace,
 
and
 
joy
 
of
 
Christ
 
in
 
our
 
lives,
 
they
 
will
 
be
 
more
 
likely
 
to
 
listen
 
to
 
what
 
we
 
have
 
to
 
say.
 
 Application: ● How can you deepen your understanding of the gospel to be ready 
to
 
defend
 
it?
 ● What are some common objections or questions you’ve 
encountered
 
that
 
you
 
could
 
prepare
 
answers
 
for?
 
 6. Trusting God for the Outcome At the end of the day, we must trust that God is the one who opens hearts. 
Our
 
responsibility
 
is
 
to
 
present
 
the
 
truth
 
with
 
clarity
 
and
 
love,
 
but
 
it
 
is
 
the
 232

Holy Spirit who convicts and draws people to Christ. Therefore, while we 
are
 
called
 
to
 
be
 
faithful
 
in
 
defending
 
our
 
faith,
 
we
 
must
 
also
 
rest
 
in
 
God’s
 
sovereignty
 
and
 
trust
 
Him
 
with
 
the
 
results.
 Application: ● How can you cultivate trust in God’s sovereignty in your 
conversations
 
about
 
the
 
gospel?
 ● Are you willing to trust God with the outcome, even if someone 
does
 
not
 
respond
 
immediately?
 
 Conclusion: A Call to Boldness and Love Defending the faith is not just for theologians or apologists; it is for every 
believer.
 
We
 
are
 
all
 
called
 
to
 
be
 
ready
 
to
 
give
 
an
 
answer
 
for
 
the
 
hope
 
within
 
us.
 
As
 
we
 
do
 
so,
 
we
 
must
 
remember
 
to
 
approach
 
others
 
with
 
meekness,
 
humility,
 
and
 
reverence
 
for
 
God.
 
Apologetics
 
is
 
not
 
about
 
winning
 
arguments
 
but
 
about
 
pointing
 
people
 
to
 
the
 
truth
 
of
 
the
 
gospel
 
and
 
the
 
hope
 
found
 
in
 
Christ.
 Let us commit to being faithful witnesses of the gospel, ready to defend 
our
 
faith
 
with
 
love,
 
truth,
 
and
 
the
 
power
 
of
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit.
